01news
Over 900 Oracle E-Business instances exposed to ongoing attacks
bleepingcomputer.com · 01 Jul 2026 12:30 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGScisaclopcve-2026-46817defusednational-association-of-insurance-commissionersnissannottingham-universityoracleoracle-e-business-suiteoracle-paymentsoracle-peoplesoft-suiteshadowservershinyhuntersunauthenticated-remote-code-executionunited-states
95
In plain words

Attackers are now targeting a serious flaw in Oracle E-Business Suite, a business software product, and about 950 of these systems are visible on the internet. The flaw can let outsiders take over affected systems without needing an account, and Oracle has already released a fix and urged customers to install it. This matters because Oracle business software is used by major organizations, and recent Oracle-related attacks have led to stolen data at universities, companies, and other groups worldwide.

Summary
Over 900 Oracle E-Business Suite instances are exposed online as attackers actively exploit CVE-2026-46817, a critical unauthenticated HTTP takeover flaw in Oracle Payments. Oracle patched the issue in its May 2026 update and is urging customers to apply fixes immediately.

Over 900 Oracle E-Business Suite instances have been found exposed online amid ongoing attacks exploiting CVE-2026-46817. The flaw affects the File Transmission component of the Oracle Payments product and allows unauthenticated attackers with HTTP network access to take over vulnerable systems through low-complexity attacks. Oracle patched the issue in its May 2026 Critical Security Patch Update and urged customers to patch immediately.

Defused warned that attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-46817, with the first attempts observed over the weekend against Oracle E-Business honeypots. The company said the vulnerability had no known previous exploitation and no public proof-of-concept code. Shadowserver also warned that it tracks around 950 Oracle E-Business Suite instances exposed online, though it is not known how many have been secured against this flaw.

The article places the activity in the context of recent Oracle-related exploitation. It notes that CISA recently tagged an Oracle WebLogic Server flaw as actively exploited, Oracle mitigated a PeopleSoft Suite zero-day exploited by ShinyHunters, and Clop has exploited another Oracle E-Business Suite flaw in attacks since early August 2025. CISA has added 44 vulnerabilities across Oracle products to its catalog of actively exploited flaws since November 2021, including 13 abused by ransomware gangs.

  • Defused reported exploitation even though it said there was no public proof-of-concept code and no known previous exploitation, showing that attackers had operationalized the flaw before public exploit details were available.
  • Oracle had already patched CVE-2026-46817 in May 2026, so the ongoing exposure of around 950 internet-facing Oracle E-Business Suite instances highlights a gap between patch availability and real-world remediation.
  • Oracle has not yet flagged CVE-2026-46817 as exploited in attacks, but two independent security organizations, Defused and Shadowserver, have issued warnings tied to exploitation activity and exposed instances.
  • The article places CVE-2026-46817 in a broader pattern of recent Oracle-related exploitation, including PeopleSoft attacks by ShinyHunters and Oracle E-Business Suite attacks by Clop against universities and major organizations.
Key Points
  • 01Over 900 Oracle E-Business Suite instances have been found exposed online amid ongoing attacks exploiting CVE-2026-46817.
  • 02CVE-2026-46817 affects the File Transmission component of Oracle Payments in Oracle E-Business Suite and allows unauthenticated attackers with HTTP network access to take over vulnerable systems through low-complexity attacks.
  • 03Oracle patched CVE-2026-46817 in its May 2026 Critical Security Patch Update and urged customers to patch their systems immediately.
  • 04Defused said it observed exploitation attempts against Oracle E-Business honeypots over the weekend and noted that there was no known previous exploitation or public proof-of-concept code.
  • 05Shadowserver reported tracking around 950 Oracle E-Business Suite instances exposed online, but the article says it is unknown how many have been secured against CVE-2026-46817.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a must-read because it reports active exploitation of a critical Oracle E-Business Suite flaw that can allow unauthenticated takeover, with around 950 exposed instances tracked online. The affected software is enterprise-facing and the article includes immediate remediation relevance because Oracle has already issued patches.

Reports
  • May 2026 Critical Security Patch Updateadvisory
    Oracle
    The article says Oracle patched CVE-2026-46817 in security updates released as part of this Critical Security Patch Update and urged customers to patch immediately.
  • Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalogadvisory
    CISA
    The article references CISA's catalog to state that 44 vulnerabilities across Oracle products have been added as actively exploited flaws since November 2021.
02news
FortiBleed credential-theft campaign linked to Lynx ransomware
bleepingcomputer.com · 01 Jul 2026 21:37 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSadminincisacredential-stuffingfortibleedfortigatefortigate-snifferfortinetinc-ransomlynxnextcloudnextcloud-zero-daysocradarworldwide
94
In plain words

Researchers say a large campaign stole login details from many Fortinet firewall devices and is connected to the INC and Lynx ransomware groups. The campaign reportedly targeted more than 430,000 FortiGate firewalls worldwide and placed tools on about 19,000 devices to capture sign-in information, with around 11,000 still compromised after notifications. This matters because the stolen access could help criminals break into organizations later, and some victim information appears to overlap with organizations later named on a ransomware leak site.

Summary
Researchers linked the FortiBleed credential-theft campaign, which stole credentials from tens of thousands of Fortinet devices, to the INC and Lynx ransomware operations. The findings suggest the stolen FortiGate access was being used to support future ransomware intrusions.

The FortiBleed credential-theft campaign has been linked to the INC and Lynx ransomware operations, suggesting stolen Fortinet credentials were intended to support future network intrusions. The campaign came to light after a server exposed on the internet was found containing credentials stolen from more than 73,000 Fortinet devices. Researchers found downloaded FortiGate configuration files, harvested credentials, and infrastructure used to crack password hashes and perform credential-stuffing attacks.

SOCRadar said its Threat Research Unit tied the operation to INC and Lynx after identifying a Windows server used in the FortiBleed infrastructure. The company said artifacts on that server showed the threat actor had accessed ransomware negotiation panels for both Lynx and INC, including dashboards with victim chats used during negotiations. SOCRadar also reported overlaps between victim information harvested during FortiBleed and organizations later listed on the INC ransomware leak site.

The campaign used a custom tool called “FortiGate Sniffer” on compromised FortiGate firewalls to intercept VPN credentials and other authentication data from network traffic. SOCRadar said the operation targeted more than 430,000 FortiGate firewalls worldwide, deployed traffic sniffers on about 19,000 devices, and still had around 11,000 compromised devices after impacted organizations were notified. The researchers also said they identified roughly 500 servers used by the operation, persistent backdoor accounts named “adminin,” and evidence suggesting the attackers exploited a previously undisclosed Nextcloud zero-day vulnerability.

  • The link to INC and Lynx was based on browser sessions from FortiBleed infrastructure accessing both ransomware groups’ negotiation panels, which ties the credential-theft operation to ransomware activity beyond just possession of stolen Fortinet data.
  • SOCRadar said the number of compromised devices dropped from about 19,000 to around 11,000 after impacted organizations were notified, indicating that notification and remediation efforts had a measurable effect.
  • The reported overlap between FortiBleed-harvested victim information and organizations later listed on the INC ransomware leak site suggests the stolen Fortinet credentials were not only collected at scale but also connected to later extortion activity.
  • The discovery of persistent backdoor accounts using the username “adminin” gives defenders a concrete artifact to check for on compromised systems mentioned in the investigation.
  • SOCRadar’s finding of more than 200 additional operational servers and roughly 500 servers overall shows the campaign’s infrastructure was far larger than initially understood.
Key Points
  • 01SOCRadar linked the FortiBleed credential-theft campaign to members of the INC and Lynx ransomware-as-a-service groups after analyzing a Windows server used in the campaign’s infrastructure.
  • 02Researchers found that the FortiBleed operation used a custom tool called “FortiGate Sniffer” on compromised FortiGate firewalls to intercept VPN credentials and other authentication data from network traffic.
  • 03SOCRadar said the campaign targeted more than 430,000 FortiGate firewalls worldwide and deployed traffic sniffers on about 19,000 devices, with roughly 11,000 devices still compromised after notifications.
  • 04The investigation identified more than 200 additional operational servers beyond those originally associated with the campaign and roughly 500 servers used by the operation overall.
  • 05SOCRadar said it found persistent backdoor accounts using the username “adminin” on compromised systems and evidence suggesting the attackers exploited a previously undisclosed Nextcloud zero-day vulnerability.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a high-impact, specific threat report linking the large FortiBleed credential-theft campaign to named ransomware operations, with claims of tens of thousands of compromised FortiGate devices and hundreds of operational servers. Security professionals should treat it as must-read because it involves stolen access credentials, persistent backdoor accounts, and infrastructure potentially used to fuel ransomware intrusions.

Reports
  • PRIMARYFortiBleed INC Lynx Ransomware Linkresearch
    SOCRadar Threat Research Unit
    The article says SOCRadar’s latest research ties the FortiBleed credential-theft operation directly to members of the INC and Lynx ransomware-as-a-service groups.
  • INC Ransomware Rebrand to Lynxresearch
    Palo Alto Networks Unit 42
    The article cites security researchers’ belief that Lynx is a rebrand of the INC ransomware gang rather than a new extortion group.
03news
Progress Kemp LoadMaster Pre-Auth RCE Flaw Faces Active Exploitation Attempts
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 13:56 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGScanadacve-2024-1212cve-2026-8037esentireesentire-trukemp-loadmasteros-command-injectionprogressremote-code-executionwatchtowr-labs
94
In plain words

A serious flaw in Progress Kemp LoadMaster devices is now being actively targeted, according to eSentire, after details and a public example of how to abuse it became available. The issue can let someone with no login run commands on a vulnerable LoadMaster appliance, which could give them control over the device. eSentire said the attempts it saw failed and did not lead to further activity, but the stakes are high because successful attacks could let outsiders take over affected appliances without valid credentials.

Summary
Progress Kemp LoadMaster’s critical pre-auth command injection flaw CVE-2026-8037 is now seeing active exploitation attempts, though observed attacks have reportedly failed so far. With PoC details available, exposed appliances should be patched urgently.

A critical security flaw affecting Progress Kemp LoadMaster is seeing active exploitation attempts, according to an advisory from eSentire’s Threat Response Unit. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-8037, has a CVSS score of 9.6 and is described as an operating system command injection flaw. eSentire said exploitation activity began on June 29, 2026, and observed attempts came from three listed IP addresses.

Progress said the flaw affects the LoadMaster API and allows an unauthenticated attacker with permissions to execute arbitrary commands by exploiting unsanitized input. watchTowr Labs described the issue as rooted in the escape_quotes() function, which failed to properly null-terminate sanitized strings and caused an out-of-bounds read into adjacent heap memory. The article says attackers could use specially crafted requests to the /accessv2 endpoint to manipulate heap memory and enable command injection.

Successful exploitation could allow an unauthenticated attacker to run arbitrary commands on a vulnerable LoadMaster appliance without valid credentials. eSentire noted that the exploitation attempts it observed failed, so no post-compromise activity occurred. The article warns that the availability of a proof-of-concept exploit and detailed technical information is expected to drive malicious activity against CVE-2026-8037 in the immediate future.

  • The article provides three source IP addresses tied to observed exploitation attempts, giving defenders concrete indicators to check in logs: 192.42.116[.]58, 192.42.116[.]105, and 146.70.139[.]154.
  • The /accessv2 endpoint is specifically identified as part of the exploit path, making requests to that endpoint a notable focus for investigation on exposed LoadMaster appliances.
  • The flaw is not only a simple input-filtering issue; watchTowr Labs linked it to improper null-termination in escape_quotes(), which can cause an out-of-bounds read into adjacent heap memory before command injection becomes possible.
  • This is the second Progress Kemp LoadMaster operating system command injection flaw cited in the article as facing active exploitation efforts, following CVE-2024-1212.
Key Points
  • 01eSentire's Threat Response Unit reported active exploitation attempts targeting CVE-2026-8037, a critical Progress Kemp LoadMaster vulnerability, beginning on June 29, 2026.
  • 02CVE-2026-8037 is an operating system command injection flaw with a CVSS score of 9.6 that can allow an unauthenticated attacker to execute arbitrary commands on susceptible LoadMaster appliances.
  • 03Progress said the vulnerability exists in the LoadMaster API and is caused by unsanitized input that can be exploited by an unauthenticated attacker with permissions to execute arbitrary commands.
  • 04watchTowr Labs analyzed the flaw as involving improper null-termination in the escape_quotes() function, enabling specially crafted requests to the /accessv2 endpoint to manipulate heap memory and achieve command injection.
  • 05eSentire said the exploitation attempts it observed failed and produced no post-compromise activity, but warned that the availability of a proof-of-concept exploit and technical details is expected to drive further malicious activity.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a must-read because it reports active exploitation attempts against a critical pre-auth remote code execution flaw in Progress Kemp LoadMaster, with a CVSS score of 9.6. The article also notes that PoC exploit details are available and provides concrete indicators and technical details relevant to defenders.

Reports
  • PRIMARYProgress Kemp LoadMaster Vulnerability Targeted: CVE-2026-8037advisory
    eSentire Threat Response Unit
    The article cites eSentire's advisory as the source reporting active exploitation attempts against CVE-2026-8037.
  • LoadMaster Critical Security Bulletin June 2026 CVE-2026-8037 CVE-2026-33691advisory
    Progress
    The article quotes Progress's advisory describing the unauthenticated command injection vulnerability in the LoadMaster API.
  • Progress Kemp LoadMaster Flaw Could Let Attackers Execute Arbitrary Commandsresearch
    watchTowr Labs
    The article references a technical analysis by watchTowr Labs explaining the escape_quotes() flaw and how crafted requests to /accessv2 can enable command injection.
04research
PolinRider: North Korea-Linked Supply Chain Campaign Expands Across Open Source Ecosystems
socket.dev · 01 Jul 2026 17:53 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGS7spanchrome-extensionscontagious-interviewdev-popperfamous-chollimagit-history-rewritinggithubgo-modulesnorth-koreanpmomnistealerpackagistpolinridervisual-studio-code-tasksxpos587
94
In plain words

Researchers found that a North Korea-linked campaign called PolinRider is secretly adding harmful code to software packages and browser-related tools that developers may download and use. The people most directly affected are developers and teams who installed the poisoned versions, because the code can steal passwords, source code access, cloud keys, and other work credentials from their computers. This matters because the attackers are abusing real software projects and hiding their changes to make them look normal, which can spread the risk from one developer’s machine into company code, package accounts, and build systems.

Summary
Socket researchers found the North Korea-linked PolinRider supply chain campaign has expanded beyond npm into Packagist, Go modules, and Chrome extensions, with 162 malicious artifacts across 108 packages and extensions. The campaign compromises legitimate repositories, hides JavaScript loaders in config files or fake font files, and can deliver malware such as DEV#POPPER and OmniStealer, so affected developer environments should be treated as compromised.

Socket Threat Research Team identified 162 malicious release artifacts across 108 packages and extensions linked to PolinRider, a supply chain campaign associated with North Korean threat actors and the broader Contagious Interview / Famous Chollima activity cluster. The campaign has expanded beyond npm into Packagist, Go modules, and Chrome extensions, with compromise traces in 80 Go modules, 10 Packagist packages, and one Chrome extension. Socket says the campaign remains active and that new malicious packages are likely to continue appearing as threat actors compromise maintainer accounts, modify legitimate repositories, and publish infected versions where they have registry access.

The campaign’s core methods include planting obfuscated JavaScript loaders in legitimate repositories, hiding code through whitespace padding or fake .woff2 font files, and triggering execution through developer tooling such as VS Code task files. The threat actors also use Git history rewriting, including force pushes and anti-dated commits, to make malicious changes appear older and less suspicious. The article highlights examples involving the Xpos587 GitHub account and the 7span organization, where visible repository history could appear benign while GitHub Activity logs showed signs of later modification.

Once deobfuscated, the loader reaches out to blockchain and public RPC infrastructure, including TRON, Aptos, and BNB Smart Chain services, retrieves encrypted second-stage payload material, decrypts it with embedded XOR keys, and executes it with eval(). Observed payloads include DEV#POPPER and OmniStealer, but the article says the loader-based design should be treated as capable of delivering additional malware. Teams that installed affected package or extension versions are advised to treat the environment as compromised, preserve forensic artifacts, rebuild from known-good lockfiles, rotate exposed secrets from a clean machine, and audit developer workstations and repositories for hidden execution paths.

  • Visible GitHub commit history is not reliable for this campaign because the attackers used force pushes and anti-dated commits, so defenders need to review GitHub Activity logs and package release metadata instead of trusting the repository landing page.
  • Partial cleanup can leave an organization exposed because 7span maintainers removed fake .woff2 payloads but some obfuscated JavaScript hidden in configuration files remained present.
  • Registry impact depends on which credentials the attackers obtained, as the article observed malicious Go module releases from Xpos587 but no malicious PyPI releases from that maintainer and no corresponding malicious npm releases from 7span.
  • VS Code folder-open tasks are a high-value audit target because PolinRider can turn files that appear to be static font assets into code execution paths by running fake .woff2 files with Node.js.
Key Points
  • 01Socket Threat Research Team identified 162 malicious release artifacts across 108 unique packages and extensions in npm, Packagist, Go modules, and Chrome extensions linked to the North Korea-associated PolinRider campaign.
  • 02PolinRider threat actors compromise legitimate GitHub repositories, plant obfuscated JavaScript loaders, and use Git history rewriting, force pushes, and anti-dated commits to make malicious changes appear older and less suspicious.
  • 03Observed PolinRider payloads are hidden either in configuration files such as vite.config.js or in fake .woff2 font files that can be executed through VS Code task files configured to run when a folder opens.
  • 04After deobfuscation, the loader contacts blockchain and public RPC infrastructure including TRON, Aptos, and BNB Smart Chain services, retrieves encrypted second-stage payload material, decrypts it with embedded XOR keys, and executes it with eval().
  • 05The article advises teams that installed affected package or extension versions to treat the environment as compromised, preserve forensic artifacts, rebuild from known-good lockfiles, rotate exposed secrets from a clean machine, and audit developer machines and repositories for hidden execution paths.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a must-read because it reports an active North Korea-linked supply chain campaign compromising legitimate open source repositories and publishing malicious artifacts across npm, Packagist, Go modules, and Chrome extensions. It includes concrete affected accounts, ecosystems, payload behaviors, and actionable guidance for teams that may have installed impacted packages.

Reports
  • North Korean APT Malware Analysis: DEV#POPPER RAT and OmniStealer: Everyday I’m Shufflinresearch
    eSentire
    The article cites this analysis when describing observed follow-on payloads including DEV#POPPER and OmniStealer and their capabilities.
05advisory
Adobe Patches 7 CVSS 10.0 Flaws in ColdFusion and Campaign Classic
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 15:25 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSadobeadobe-campaign-classicadobe-coldfusioncve-2026-48276cve-2026-48277cve-2026-48281cve-2026-48282cve-2026-48283cve-2026-48286cve-2026-48316improper-input-validationincorrect-authorizationpath-traversalunrestricted-file-upload
93
  • └
    advisory
    Adobe patches seven max severity ColdFusion, Campaign flaws
    bleepingcomputer.com · 01 Jul 2026 07:34 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
    TAGSacrobat-readeradobeadobe-campaign-classicadobe-coldfusioncisacve-2026-34621cve-2026-48276cve-2026-48277cve-2026-48281cve-2026-48282cve-2026-48286cve-2026-48316
    In plain words

    Adobe released fixes for seven very serious security problems in its ColdFusion web app platform and Campaign Classic marketing platform. The people most affected are organizations using older ColdFusion versions or on-premises Adobe Campaign Classic systems, because attackers could use these flaws to run their own code on unpatched systems without needing a user to click anything. Adobe says it has not seen these specific issues being used in real attacks, but it rated the updates as urgent because similar Adobe flaws have often been targeted, including some used by ransomware gangs.

    Summary
    Adobe released urgent patches for seven maximum-severity flaws in ColdFusion and Campaign Classic that could allow remote or arbitrary code execution, urging administrators to update quickly despite no known active exploitation. The affected products include ColdFusion 2025.9, 2023.20 and earlier, and on-premises Campaign Classic 7.4.3 build 9396 and earlier.

    Adobe released security patches for seven maximum-severity vulnerabilities in ColdFusion and Campaign Classic. The flaws can be exploited in low-complexity attacks that do not require user interaction and were assigned Adobe priority 1, indicating a high risk of being targeted. Adobe recommends administrators install the updates as soon as possible, such as within 72 hours, while also saying it is not aware of any exploits in the wild for the issues addressed.

    Six critical ColdFusion flaws affect versions 2025.9, 2023.20, and earlier, and can allow attackers without privileges to gain remote code execution on unpatched systems. The Campaign Classic vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-48286, affects versions 7.4.3 build 9396 and earlier and could lead to arbitrary code execution in the current user’s context. Adobe said CVE-2026-48286 affects on-premises Adobe Campaign instances, including fully on-premises deployments and on-premises components in hybrid deployments, while Adobe-hosted instances have already been patched.

    Adobe Chief Security Officer Aanchal Gupta also said the company will move from monthly to twice-monthly security bulletins starting July 14, 2026, with advisories published on the second and fourth Tuesday of each month. Adobe said its out-of-band response process will remain in effect for actively exploited vulnerabilities or externally discovered zero-day vulnerabilities. The article also notes that CISA has added 79 Adobe product flaws to its catalog of actively exploited vulnerabilities over the last five years, including 10 that were also abused by ransomware gangs.

    • Adobe assigned the updates priority 1 even while saying it has not seen exploitation, meaning the company still considers these flaws at high risk of being targeted and recommends installing fixes within 72 hours.
    • The Campaign Classic issue is specifically an on-premises risk, because Adobe says Adobe-hosted instances have already been patched.
    • Adobe is moving from monthly to twice-monthly security bulletins starting July 14, 2026, to deploy security updates faster.
    • CISA has added 79 Adobe product flaws to its catalog of actively exploited vulnerabilities over the last five years, including 10 that were also abused by ransomware gangs.
    Key Points
    • 01Adobe released security patches for seven maximum-severity vulnerabilities in ColdFusion and Campaign Classic.
    • 02The vulnerabilities can be exploited in low-complexity attacks that do not require user interaction and were assigned Adobe priority 1.
    • 03Adobe said it is not aware of any exploits in the wild for the issues addressed in these updates.
    • 04The ColdFusion flaws affect versions 2025.9, 2023.20, and earlier and can allow attackers without privileges to gain remote code execution on unpatched systems.
    • 05The Campaign Classic flaw CVE-2026-48286 affects on-premises versions 7.4.3 build 9396 and earlier and could lead to arbitrary code execution in the current user's context.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This is a specific, urgent patch event covering maximum-severity Adobe ColdFusion and Campaign Classic flaws that can allow code execution without user interaction. Adobe assigned the updates priority 1 and recommends rapid installation, though it says it is not aware of active exploitation for these issues.

    Reports
    • PRIMARYSecurity update available for Adobe ColdFusion | APSB26-68advisory
      Adobe
      The article cites Adobe's ColdFusion security advisory as the source for affected ColdFusion versions and the related CVEs.
    • PRIMARYSecurity update available for Adobe Campaign | APSB26-69advisory
      Adobe
      The article cites Adobe's Campaign Classic security advisory for CVE-2026-48286, affected versions, and deployment scope.
    • Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalogadvisory
      Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency
      The article references CISA's catalog to state that 79 Adobe product flaws have been added as actively exploited vulnerabilities over the last five years.
In plain words

Adobe released fixes for serious security problems in Adobe ColdFusion and Adobe Campaign Classic, some of which could let an attacker run their own code on affected systems. The people affected are organizations using the listed ColdFusion versions or on-premise Adobe Campaign Classic installations, while Adobe-hosted Campaign systems have already been updated. Adobe says it has not seen anyone actively using these problems yet, but it is moving to more frequent security updates because AI is making it faster for both defenders and attackers to find flaws.

Summary
Adobe released urgent patches for multiple critical flaws in ColdFusion and Adobe Campaign Classic, including seven CVSS 10.0 vulnerabilities that could allow arbitrary code execution. The company says there is no evidence of exploitation in the wild, but affected on-premise systems should be updated immediately.

Adobe released patches for multiple maximum-severity security flaws affecting Adobe ColdFusion and Adobe Campaign Classic. The ColdFusion updates address critical and important vulnerabilities that could lead to arbitrary code execution, privilege escalation, arbitrary file system read, and security feature bypass. The issues were fixed in ColdFusion 2023 Update 21 and ColdFusion 2025 Update 10.

The ColdFusion flaws include CVE-2026-48276 and CVE-2026-48283, which involve unrestricted upload of files with dangerous types and have CVSS scores of 10.0. Other CVSS 10.0 ColdFusion issues include improper input validation vulnerabilities tracked as CVE-2026-48277, CVE-2026-48281, and CVE-2026-48316, as well as a path traversal vulnerability tracked as CVE-2026-48282. Adobe also fixed CVE-2026-48313, a path traversal issue that could allow arbitrary file system read, and CVE-2026-48315, an improper input validation issue that could lead to privilege escalation.

Adobe also patched CVE-2026-48286 in Adobe Campaign Classic, a CVSS 10.0 incorrect authorization flaw that could allow arbitrary code execution. The issue affects ACC v7 version 7.4.3 build 9396 and earlier on Windows and Linux, but only for on-premise instances and on-premise components in hybrid deployments; Adobe-hosted instances have already been updated. Adobe said it has not found any exploits in the wild for the issues, and it also announced plans to publish security bulletins twice monthly starting July 14, 2026, citing accelerated vulnerability discovery using AI models.

  • Adobe Campaign Classic exposure is limited to on-premise deployments and on-premise components in hybrid deployments, while Adobe-hosted instances have already been updated and require no action.
  • Adobe is moving from monthly to twice-monthly security bulletins starting July 14, 2026, because it says AI-accelerated vulnerability discovery is compressing the time between public disclosure and active exploitation from days to hours.
  • The ColdFusion update is especially urgent because it fixes multiple separate CVSS 10.0 paths to arbitrary code execution, including dangerous file uploads, improper input validation, and path traversal.
  • Although Adobe reported no known exploitation in the wild, its own stated concern about faster exploitation after disclosure makes prompt patching particularly actionable for affected ColdFusion and on-premise Campaign Classic users.
Key Points
  • 01Adobe released patches for multiple maximum-severity security flaws affecting Adobe ColdFusion and Adobe Campaign Classic.
  • 02The ColdFusion update fixes critical and important issues that could lead to arbitrary code execution, privilege escalation, arbitrary file system read, and security feature bypass.
  • 03ColdFusion flaws addressed include several CVSS 10.0 vulnerabilities involving unrestricted file upload, improper input validation, and path traversal.
  • 04Adobe Campaign Classic flaw CVE-2026-48286 has a CVSS score of 10.0 and affects on-premise ACC v7 version 7.4.3 build 9396 and earlier on Windows and Linux.
  • 05Adobe said it has not found any exploits in the wild for the issues addressed in these updates.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a must-read because it reports specific critical patches for Adobe ColdFusion and Adobe Campaign Classic, including seven CVSS 10.0 flaws that could allow arbitrary code execution. Even though Adobe says there is no known exploitation in the wild, affected on-premise systems require prompt updates.

Reports

    Adobe
    The article cites Adobe's ColdFusion security advisory as the source for the listed vulnerabilities and fixed versions.
  • PRIMARYAdobe Campaign Classic Security Update APSB26-69advisory
    Adobe
    The article cites Adobe's Campaign Classic security advisory for details on CVE-2026-48286 and the patched ACC v7 build.
06news
Azure CLI Password Spray Hits at Least 78 Microsoft Accounts in 81M+ Attempts
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 05:46 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSas32167chinaconditional-access-policieshuntresslshiy-llcmicrosoftmicrosoft-azure-climicrosoft-entra-idoauth-2-0password-sprayingresource-owner-password-credentialsunited-states
92
  • └
    news
    Hackers target Microsoft 365 accounts with 81 million login attempts
    bleepingcomputer.com · 01 Jul 2026 16:38 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
    TAGSas32167azure-clihuntresslshiy-llcmicrosoftmicrosoft-365microsoft-azuremicrosoft-conditional-accessoauthpassword-sprayingresource-owner-password-credentials
    In plain words

    Hackers tried more than 81 million Microsoft 365 sign-ins over two weeks using usernames and passwords exposed in past breaches. Huntress said 78 Microsoft accounts at 64 organizations were compromised, including some where extra sign-in checks were turned on but did not apply to the way the hackers logged in. This matters because businesses may think their accounts are protected, while gaps in their sign-in rules can still let attackers get into work email and cloud accounts.

    Summary
    A large password-spraying campaign generated over 81 million Microsoft 365 login attempts and compromised 78 accounts across 64 organizations. Many victims had MFA, but misconfigured Conditional Access policies failed to cover Azure CLI/ROPC authentication, letting attackers bypass MFA.

    An aggressive password-spraying campaign targeted Microsoft 365 environments and generated more than 81 million login attempts over a two-week period. Huntress observed the campaign against its customers between June 12 and 26 and confirmed that 78 Microsoft accounts across 64 organizations were compromised. The company also reported a more than 155-fold increase in password-spraying attacks, with organizations now averaging 1,964 failed login attempts per tenant each month.

    The threat actor tried to authenticate through Microsoft’s Azure command-line interface using still-valid username and password combinations exposed in past breaches. Once a valid pair was found, the attacker used the Resource Owner Password Credentials OAuth mechanism, which Huntress said can bypass multi-factor authentication in many environments when Conditional Access policies are insecurely configured. Huntress highlighted misconfigurations such as MFA not applying to all cloud apps, only covering selected user groups, depending on trusted locations, or remaining in report-only mode.

    • Having MFA enabled was not enough for several impacted organizations because their Conditional Access policies did not apply to the specific ROPC authentication flow used in the campaign.
    • MFA policies limited to specific applications, selected user groups, or untrusted locations can leave exploitable gaps even when organizations believe they have MFA coverage.
    • Conditional Access policies set to report-only mode provided no protection in this campaign because they were not actually enforced.
    • ROPC is especially risky in this context because it sends the password directly to the token endpoint without an interactive MFA prompt.
    • Huntress observed a more than 155-fold increase in password-spraying attacks, indicating a sharp rise in this type of activity against its monitored organizations.
    Key Points
    • 01An aggressive password-spraying campaign targeting Microsoft 365 environments generated more than 81 million login attempts over a two-week period.
    • 02The threat actor attempted to authenticate through Microsoft’s Azure command-line interface using still-valid username and password combinations exposed in past breaches.
    • 03Huntress observed the campaign between June 12 and 26 and confirmed that 78 Microsoft accounts across 64 organizations were compromised.
    • 04Huntress said many compromised businesses had multi-factor authentication configured through Conditional Access policies, but those policies did not cover the ROPC authentication flow used by the attackers.
    • 05Huntress reported that the activity originated from an IPv6 range owned by LSHIY LLC and that it had not received a response after reporting the findings through the company’s abuse portal.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This is a specific, recent Microsoft 365 password-spraying campaign with broad relevance, involving 81 million login attempts and confirmed account compromises across 64 organizations. It is especially important because it shows how MFA can fail when Conditional Access policies do not cover the authentication flow attackers use.

    Reports
    • PRIMARYLSHIY Password Spray Attackresearch
      Huntress
      The article cites Huntress's analysis as the source for details on the Microsoft 365 password-spraying campaign, compromised accounts, and Conditional Access policy weaknesses.
In plain words

Researchers say someone tried to sign in to Microsoft accounts more than 81 million times through Azure’s command-line tool and succeeded in taking over at least 78 accounts at 64 organizations. The attackers used old leaked username-and-password pairs, and some organizations were affected even though they had extra sign-in protections because those protections were not set up to cover this kind of login. This matters because it shows that simply having added security checks is not enough if they are not applied broadly and correctly.

Summary
Huntress says attackers made over 81 million Azure CLI login attempts and compromised at least 78 Microsoft accounts by using old breached credentials and the deprecated ROPC OAuth flow. The key takeaway is that MFA and Conditional Access can be bypassed if not applied to all users, cloud apps, and client app types, including Azure CLI logins.

Cybersecurity researchers at Huntress warned of a massive, ongoing, automated password spray attack aimed at Microsoft’s Azure command-line interface. Between June 12 and June 26, the threat actor made more than 81 million login attempts and compromised at least 78 Microsoft accounts across 64 organizations. Huntress said the targeting appeared to be based on password prevalence in compromised password combo lists, rather than a specific business type or industry.

The activity largely originated from the IPv6 range 2a0a:d683::/32, which is controlled by internet infrastructure provider LSHIY LLC. Some IP addresses resolved to the United States, while a few resolved to China. Huntress said the campaign was part of a larger wave of credential spray attacks across several ASNs, with credential spray volume across its customer base increasing by more than 155 times.

A notable technical detail is that the attackers used the deprecated Resource Owner Password Credentials OAuth flow, known as ROPC, to bypass some Conditional Access Policy protections. Microsoft recommends against using ROPC and says it is incompatible with multi-factor authentication. Huntress said organizations should require MFA for all users, all cloud apps, and all client app types, restrict Azure CLI for non-admin users, and prioritize response based on credential validity.

  • The campaign shows that Conditional Access policies can leave a major gap when MFA is scoped only to certain apps, user groups, or locations instead of covering All Users, All Cloud Apps, and All Client App types.
  • The use of the deprecated ROPC flow was especially significant because it can avoid the authorization endpoint where some Conditional Access policies are enforced, allowing attackers to get in even where MFA existed.
  • The attacks appear to have succeeded by using old breached username-and-password combinations that had never been rotated, making credential age and validity a practical response priority.
  • The spike on June 22, when 30 identities across 23 businesses were impacted, indicates the campaign’s success rate was not steady and could escalate sharply within a single day.
  • Eight affected businesses had no MFA policy at all, but Huntress emphasized that the broader lesson is not that MFA fails, but that incomplete MFA coverage creates exploitable openings.
Key Points
  • 01Huntress warned of a massive, ongoing, automated password spray attack against Microsoft's Azure command-line interface that made more than 81 million login attempts between June 12 and June 26.
  • 02The campaign compromised at least 78 Microsoft accounts across 64 organizations, with targeting based on password prevalence in compromised password combo lists rather than business type or industry.
  • 03The activity originated largely from the IPv6 range 2a0a:d683::/32 controlled by LSHIY LLC, with some IP addresses resolving to the United States and a few to China.
  • 04The attackers leveraged the deprecated Resource Owner Password Credentials OAuth flow to bypass some poorly configured Conditional Access policies, including cases where MFA was not applied to all cloud apps, all users, or all client app types.
  • 05Huntress advised organizations to require MFA for all users, all cloud apps, and all client app types, restrict the Azure CLI application for non-admin users, and prioritize response by credential validity.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific, ongoing in-the-wild credential attack against Microsoft Azure CLI with more than 81 million login attempts and at least 78 compromised accounts across 64 organizations. It is important for security professionals because it shows how poorly scoped MFA and Conditional Access policies can be bypassed through the deprecated ROPC flow.

Reports
  • PRIMARYLSHIY Password Spray Attackresearch
    Huntress
    The article cites Huntress as the source reporting the Azure CLI password spray campaign, including the scale of login attempts, affected accounts, and abuse of the ROPC flow.
07news
Critical Cursor Flaws Could Let Prompt Injection Escape Sandbox and Run Commands
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 14:42 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGScato-ai-labscursorcursorsandboxcurxecutecve-2026-50548cve-2026-50549duneslidemacosmcpoisonmodel-context-protocolprompt-injectionsandbox-escapezero-click
92
In plain words

Researchers found two serious flaws in Cursor, an AI tool programmers use to write code, that could let hidden instructions in something the tool reads make it run commands on the programmer’s computer without any warning or approval. Anyone using a Cursor version before 3.0 is affected, and the company has already released a fix. This matters because a successful attack could give someone control of a developer’s machine and access to cloud or work accounts connected through the editor, even from one normal-looking request.

Summary
Two critical Cursor flaws dubbed DuneSlide could let hidden prompt-injection instructions escape the editor’s sandbox and run commands on a developer’s machine without any user approval. The issues affect all versions before Cursor 3.0, so users should update immediately.

Cato AI Labs found two critical flaws in Cursor, an AI code editor, that could let a single ordinary-looking prompt escape the editor’s sandbox and run commands on a developer’s computer. The flaws are named DuneSlide and tracked as CVE-2026-50548 and CVE-2026-50549, both rated 9.8 under CVSS 3.1 or 9.3 under CVSS 4.0. All Cursor versions before 3.0 are affected, and Cursor 3.0, released April 2, includes fixes.

The attack works through prompt injection, where hidden instructions are planted in something the Cursor agent reads, such as a connected Model Context Protocol service or a web search result. The article describes the attack as zero-click because it requires no user approval or click. Both flaws use the same general approach: get the agent to write a file it should not be allowed to write, then use that write to turn off the sandbox.

CVE-2026-50548 abuses Cursor’s working_directory parameter to add an attacker-chosen path to the allowed-write list, while CVE-2026-50549 abuses a failed symlink safety check that falls back to trusting an in-project path. In both cases, the attacker can target files such as Cursor’s sandbox helper, so later commands run without sandbox restrictions. The article says there is no sign the flaws have been used in real attacks, but successful exploitation could give control of the developer’s machine and any cloud or SaaS workspaces the editor is signed into.

  • The flaws show that Cursor’s 2.x sandbox, which was introduced as a defense after earlier prompt-to-command-execution bugs, could itself be bypassed by getting the agent to write a single unauthorized file.
  • The attack path did not require a malicious user to type into Cursor directly, because the hidden instructions could be planted in external content the agent reads on the user’s behalf, such as an MCP-connected service or web search result.
  • Cursor initially rejected Cato’s reports because it said misuse of MCP servers was outside its threat model, but later reopened the reports, triaged them, and shipped fixes in Cursor 3.0.
  • The article notes a recurring pattern across multiple Cursor issues in which poisoned prompts defeat different guardrails, suggesting that patching individual bugs has not eliminated the broader risk for coding agents that read external content.
Key Points
  • 01Cato AI Labs found two critical flaws in Cursor, named DuneSlide and tracked as CVE-2026-50548 and CVE-2026-50549, that could let a prompt injection escape the editor’s sandbox and run commands on a developer’s computer.
  • 02Both vulnerabilities affect every Cursor version before 3.0, and Cursor 3.0, released April 2, includes fixes for both issues.
  • 03The attack can be triggered by hidden instructions inside content the Cursor agent reads, such as a connected Model Context Protocol service or a web search result, without requiring a user click or approval.
  • 04CVE-2026-50548 abuses Cursor’s working_directory parameter to add an attacker-chosen path to the allowed-write list, while CVE-2026-50549 abuses a symlink safety-check fallback that can trust an in-project path when destination verification fails.
  • 05Once the sandbox is neutralized, later commands can run as the user, potentially giving control of the developer’s machine and any cloud or SaaS workspaces the editor is signed into.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a high-impact report on two critical Cursor vulnerabilities that could allow zero-click command execution through prompt injection and affect all versions before 3.0. Even though there is no known exploitation in the wild, the combination of critical severity, patch availability, and Cursor’s use by major organizations makes it important for security professionals to read.

Reports
  • PRIMARYDuneSlide: Two Critical RCE Vulnerabilitiesresearch
    Cato AI Labs
    The article says Cato AI Labs found the two Cursor flaws, named them DuneSlide, and presented them as research.
  • GHSA-3v8f-48vw-3mjxadvisory
    Cursor
    The article says Cursor published its own advisory for the symlink bug.
  • CVE-2026-50549advisory
    National Vulnerability Database
    The article notes that the NVD record for the Cursor symlink bug is live.
08advisory
Emerging Threat: (CVE-2026-55957) Apache Tomcat Authentication Bypass via JNDIRealm GSSAPI Binds
cycognito.com · 01 Jul 2026 09:18 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSactive-directoryapache-software-foundationapache-tomcatcve-2026-55957cycognitogssapijndirealmkerberosldap
92
In plain words

The article describes a serious flaw in Apache Tomcat that can let someone get into certain protected web applications without the right password. It affects Tomcat systems that use an outside company directory, such as LDAP or Active Directory, for sign-in in a specific setup. This matters because older internal business applications are often forgotten but may still be reachable from outside, and a successful bypass could expose whatever private data or functions those applications protect.

Summary
CVE-2026-55957 is a critical Apache Tomcat authentication bypass affecting JNDIRealm configurations that use GSSAPI LDAP binds, allowing pre-authentication access without the correct password. Organizations should upgrade to Tomcat 11.0.5, 10.1.37, or 9.0.101 and urgently identify any exposed directory-integrated Tomcat instances.

CVE-2026-55957 is a critical Apache Tomcat authentication bypass affecting JNDIRealm when it is configured to authenticate LDAP binds using GSSAPI. The vulnerability has a CVSS v3.1 base score of 9.8, while Apache classifies it as “Important” because exploitation depends on a specific deployment configuration. Exploitation is pre-authentication and can allow an attacker to authenticate without the correct password.

The affected condition applies to Tomcat instances that delegate authentication to an LDAP or Active Directory server through JNDIRealm with GSSAPI selected as the bind mechanism. A successful bypass grants access according to the directory-backed identity used inside the application, exposing resources protected by that authentication boundary. Affected versions include Tomcat 11.0.0-M1 through 11.0.4, 10.1.0-M1 through 10.1.36, 9.0.0.M1 through 9.0.100, 8.5.0 through 8.5.100, and 7.0.0 through 7.0.109.

Apache has released fixed versions for supported branches: Tomcat 11.0.5, 10.1.37, and 9.0.101. The article says Apache lists no workaround other than upgrading, and organizations on end-of-life 8.5.x or 7.0.x branches should treat migration to a supported branch as part of remediation. Recommended actions include inventorying affected JNDIRealm and GSSAPI configurations, restricting network access, monitoring directory server logs, auditing recent access, disabling GSSAPI-authenticated binds where immediate upgrade is not feasible, and confirming fixes in third-party platforms that bundle Tomcat.

  • Apache rates the issue as “Important” despite its 9.8 critical CVSS score because exploitation depends on a specific JNDIRealm configuration using GSSAPI-authenticated binds.
  • The article highlights Consumer Discretionary organizations as the largest observed exposure group at 57.1%, linking this to distributed franchise, subsidiary, and e-commerce environments with historically separate directory integrations.
  • The risk is amplified because directory-integrated Tomcat instances were often built for internal use but may later become reachable through VPN gateways, reverse proxies, or forgotten firewall rules.
  • Apache lists no workaround other than upgrading, so organizations running end-of-life Tomcat 8.5.x or 7.0.x are expected to migrate to a supported branch as part of remediation.
Key Points
  • 01CVE-2026-55957 is an Apache Tomcat authentication bypass in JNDIRealm when it is configured to authenticate LDAP binds using GSSAPI.
  • 02The vulnerability is pre-authentication and can allow an attacker to authenticate without supplying the correct password, gaining access based on the affected directory-backed identity.
  • 03Affected releases include Apache Tomcat 11.0.0-M1 through 11.0.4, 10.1.0-M1 through 10.1.36, 9.0.0.M1 through 9.0.100, 8.5.0 through 8.5.100, and 7.0.0 through 7.0.109.
  • 04Apache Software Foundation has released patched versions for supported branches: Tomcat 11.0.5, 10.1.37, and 9.0.101.
  • 05The article recommends inventorying Tomcat instances using JNDIRealm with GSSAPI binds, restricting network access, monitoring directory logs, auditing recent access, disabling GSSAPI-authenticated binds where upgrades are not immediately feasible, and confirming fixes in third-party platforms that bundle Tomcat.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a critical authentication bypass in widely deployed Apache Tomcat with patched versions available, and exploitation is pre-authentication for affected JNDIRealm GSSAPI configurations. Although the vulnerable setup is configuration-specific, the potential impact on directory-backed enterprise applications makes it important for security professionals to prioritize.

Should Readⓘ 18 items
01research
ARToken: Inside an EvilTokens affiliate panel targeting Microsoft 365
blog.talosintelligence.com · 01 Jul 2026 10:00 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSartbrowserartokenartsenderbusiness-email-compromisecisco-taloscloudflare-workersdevice-code-phishingeviltokensmicrosoft-365microsoft-sharepointprimary-refresh-tokenspearphishing-linkunited-statesx-antibot-tokenxor-encryption
89
  • └
    news
    22nd June – Threat Intelligence Report
    research.checkpoint.com · 01 Jul 2026 11:29 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
    TAGSbioshocking-aichinadcloud-uni-appdevice-code-phishingeviltokensfortibleedfulcrumsecicarusmiraioauth-token-theftrussiastockstayturlaukraineunited-states
    In plain words

    This report summarizes recent cyber incidents, including exposed personal data from Texas hunting and fishing license customers, stolen health information from iRhythm, compromised customer sales data through Klue, and harmful updates sent through WordPress plugins. It also describes new scams and weaknesses involving AI tools, network devices, and business software that attackers are using to steal passwords, files, and other private information. In the real world, this matters because ordinary people, patients, government customers, companies, and public agencies can have sensitive information taken or services tampered with when trusted vendors, apps, or devices are compromised.

    Summary
    Check Point’s 22 June threat intelligence roundup highlights major breaches, AI-enabled phishing and agent hijacking, actively exploited vulnerabilities in Cisco, Ubiquiti and AI platforms, and large-scale campaigns such as FortiBleed and Turla-linked StockStay. The key takeaway is that attackers are rapidly combining supply-chain compromise, stolen credentials, AI abuse, and unpatched infrastructure to steal data at scale.

    Check Point’s 22nd June Threat Intelligence Report summarizes recent attacks, breaches, AI threats, vulnerabilities, and threat intelligence findings. Reported incidents include a Texas Parks and Wildlife Department third-party breach affecting 3,087,721 hunting and fishing license customers, a ShapedPlugin supply chain attack through official WordPress plugin updates, and a cyberattack on iRhythm Technologies involving stolen protected health information and other data. Klue also confirmed a breach in which compromised legacy integration credentials were used to steal OAuth tokens connected to customer Salesforce environments.

    The report highlights AI-related threats including EvilTokens, an AI-powered phishing-as-a-service operation that abuses device-code authentication to steal Microsoft 365 tokens. It also describes a fake AI skill that hijacked more than 26,000 AI agents after appearing clean during marketplace approval, and BioShocking AI, a technique shown to trick agentic browsers into bypassing guardrails. These examples show attackers using trusted platforms, AI workflows, and browser-like agents to reach credentials, user data, and connected services.

    The vulnerability section covers exploited or patched flaws in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, Dify, Ubiquiti UniFi OS, and Langflow. Cisco addressed CVE-2026-20245 after attackers exploited it as a zero-day for months, while Ubiquiti flaws were reportedly exploited in Mirai botnet activity and Langflow flaws were used to run victim pipelines, extract embedded API keys, deploy malware, and steal cloud credentials. The report also summarizes campaigns including FortiBleed, Turla-linked StockStay, DCloud Uni-App scam infrastructure, and FulcrumSec cloud extortion activity.

    • Several incidents involved trusted third parties or integrations, including Texas Parks and Wildlife’s license vendor, ShapedPlugin’s official updater, iRhythm’s third-party-hosted applications, and Klue’s legacy integration credentials.
    • AI-related risks in the report were not limited to fake content; they included stolen Microsoft 365 tokens, hijacked AI agents, and agentic browsers being tricked into exposing credentials and user data.
    • The Klue breach shows that compromised legacy integration credentials can still create current risk by enabling theft of OAuth tokens connected to customer Salesforce environments.
    • The Langflow activity highlights that attackers targeted both remote code execution and flow ID enumeration to run victim pipelines and extract embedded API keys.
    • The FortiBleed campaign demonstrates that compromised network devices can be repurposed as passive credential collectors across many protocols rather than used only for direct disruption.
    Key Points
    • 01Texas Parks and Wildlife Department was affected by a third-party data breach involving its license system vendor that exposed driver’s license information, passport numbers, emails, phone numbers, and residential addresses for 3,087,721 hunting and fishing license customers.
    • 02ShapedPlugin faced a supply chain attack that delivered malicious updates for three paid WordPress plugins through its official updater and installed a hidden fake WooCommerce plugin to steal credentials and modify affected websites.
    • 03Researchers detailed EvilTokens, an AI-powered phishing-as-a-service operation that abuses device-code authentication to steal Microsoft 365 tokens, with Huntress observing a 1,380% surge in device-code phishing in early 2026.
    • 04Cisco addressed CVE-2026-20245, a high-severity command injection flaw in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager that attackers exploited as a zero-day for months.
    • 05Researchers uncovered the FortiBleed campaign, which converted compromised FortiGate firewalls into passive credential stealers across 24 protocols and siphoned more than 110 million credentials.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This article is a weekly threat intelligence roundup that curates multiple unrelated breaches, AI threats, vulnerabilities, and threat actor reports rather than covering a single specific event. Per the scoring rule for digest or summary pieces, it is assigned exactly 40 despite containing several high-impact items.

    Reports
    • EvilTokens: AI-Powered Phishing Reportresearch
      Huntress
      The article references Huntress research detailing EvilTokens, an AI-powered phishing-as-a-service operation abusing device-code authentication to steal Microsoft 365 tokens.
    • BioShocking AI: Gaming the AI Browser and Escaping its Guardrailsresearch
      LayerX
      The article cites LayerX research demonstrating BioShocking AI, a technique for tricking agentic browsers into bypassing guardrails and exposing credentials and user data.
    • Understanding Langflow CVE-2026-55255 and Why Higher CVSS Vulnerabilities Aren’t Always the Most Exploitedresearch
      Sysdig
      The article references Sysdig analysis of attacks targeting Langflow through CVE-2026-55255 and CVE-2026-33017.
    • From San Pedro to Salinas: How a Chinese Framework DCloud Uni-App Powers a Global Scam Economyresearch
      Infoblox
      The article cites Infoblox research on the Chinese DCloud Uni-App framework powering scam domains since 2022.
    • The FulcrumSec Playbook: How to Detect and Stop the Group Behind the Novo Nordisk Breachresearch
      Sysdig
      The article references Sysdig analysis of the FulcrumSec cloud extortion group targeting cloud-native organizations.
In plain words

Cisco Talos found an online criminal control panel called ARToken that helps attackers trick Microsoft 365 users into giving access to their accounts, including through fake invoice messages sent to accounts-payable staff. Once a victim is fooled, the attackers can read email, send messages as that person, access SharePoint and OneDrive files, and try to keep access even after a password reset. This matters because real businesses and their employees can be used for invoice fraud, stolen documents, and further scams that appear to come from trusted coworkers or vendors.

Summary
Cisco Talos uncovered ARToken, a sophisticated EvilTokens-linked phishing-as-a-service panel that targets Microsoft 365 with device-code phishing, token theft, PRT persistence, BEC tooling, and SharePoint exfiltration. The research shows the platform has evolved into a full post-compromise operations suite with advanced anti-analysis protections and Cloudflare Workers-based lure infrastructure.

Cisco Talos identified a fully featured phishing-as-a-service operator panel branded ARToken that shares infrastructure, API contracts, and operational patterns with the EvilTokens platform. The panel targets Microsoft 365 and exposes more than 80 API endpoints for device code phishing, Primary Refresh Token persistence, email access, business email compromise operations, and SharePoint exfiltration. Talos found the management panel at dashboard-bl.pamconj[.]com, with its command-and-control API at spx.pamconj[.]com and phishing lures deployed through Cloudflare Workers.

The article describes a real lure recovered by Talos that spoofed an accounts-payable contact at a legitimate Wisconsin contractor and targeted an accounts-payable recipient at a U.S. life-sciences company. The messages used an outstanding-invoice theme and displayed what appeared to be a genuine SharePoint tenant link, while the actual link pointed to a near-identical attacker-controlled SharePoint workspace. Talos notes that SPF, DKIM, and DMARC all failed, and that the messages included light per-message mutation such as random hex strings and an inline signature image.

ARToken’s phishing kit uses a seven-layer anti-analysis system that includes user-agent checks, automation detection, browser feature fingerprinting, interaction telemetry, timing gates, and mouse-movement pattern analysis. Its payload extracts the victim email from a URL hint parameter, calls the C2 at /device/start, displays a device code, and directs the victim to microsoft.com/devicelogin. The operator toolkit also includes token refresh and sharing, Outlook inbox access, victim email sending, inbox rule creation, SharePoint and OneDrive operations, Cloudflare Workers deployment management, and a standalone Windows session browser called ARTBrowser.

  • ARToken’s use of genuine SharePoint-hosted links with look-alike tenant names lets phishing destinations inherit SharePoint’s clean reputation while still sending victims to attacker-controlled workspaces.
  • The article shows that ARToken affiliates can see and use a complete post-compromise workflow from the panel, meaning the operation is designed not just to steal access tokens but to support end-to-end business email compromise activity.
  • The phishing kit’s client-side behavioral checks are more sophisticated than the server-side anti-bot mechanism previously documented for EvilTokens, indicating that affiliates may deploy upgraded or custom anti-analysis modules independently of the core platform.
  • The presence of token import and shared access links means ARToken supports collaboration and token reuse across operators, not only tokens captured directly through its own phishing pages.
  • The kit setting persistAfterPassChange: false indicates operators are explicitly warned that ordinary refresh tokens may be revoked by password resets and that they must either exfiltrate data quickly or escalate to Primary Refresh Token persistence.
Key Points
  • 01Cisco Talos identified a phishing-as-a-service operator panel branded ARToken that shares infrastructure, API contracts, and operational patterns with the EvilTokens platform.
  • 02The ARToken panel exposes more than 80 API endpoints for device code phishing, Primary Refresh Token persistence, email access, business email compromise operations, and SharePoint exfiltration through a React-based dashboard.
  • 03Talos recovered two near-identical phishing messages that spoofed a legitimate Wisconsin contractor and targeted an accounts-payable recipient at a U.S. life-sciences company with an outstanding-invoice lure.
  • 04The ARToken phishing kit uses a seven-layer anti-analysis system that includes user-agent checks, automation detection, browser fingerprinting, interaction telemetry, timing gates, and mouse-movement pattern analysis.
  • 05The ARToken operator toolkit includes token refresh and sharing, Outlook inbox access, victim email sending, inbox rule creation, SharePoint and OneDrive file operations, Cloudflare Workers deployment management, and a standalone Windows session browser called ARTBrowser.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific, technically detailed finding about an active phishing-as-a-service platform targeting Microsoft 365, with real-world lures, infrastructure, and post-compromise capabilities documented. It is broadly relevant to security professionals because it shows how ARToken/EvilTokens supports token theft, persistence, BEC operations, and SharePoint exfiltration at scale.

Reports
  • PRIMARYAI-enabled device code phishing campaign April 2026research
    Microsoft
    The article cites Microsoft’s April 2026 publication as confirming the scale of the EvilTokens campaign, including higher success rates, AI-powered lures, and automated device registration for persistent access.
  • PRIMARYEvilTokens: An AI-Augmented Phishing-as-a-Service for Automating BEC Fraud – Part 2research
    Sekoia
    The article references Sekoia’s Part 2 research for previously documented EvilTokens second-stage capabilities, including its AI-augmented BEC pipeline and PRT persistence chain.
02advisory
Citrix Patches Six NetScaler Flaws Allowing File Read and Denial-of-Service
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 03:54 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGScitrixcve-2026-10816cve-2026-10817cve-2026-13474cve-2026-3055cve-2026-8451cve-2026-8452cve-2026-8655http-2jpmorgan-chasenetscaler-adcnetscaler-gatewaysamlwatchtowrxor-team
88
In plain words

Citrix released fixes for six problems in its NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway products, which are used by customers to manage access to services. If left unfixed in certain setups, these problems could let someone read files they should not see or make the device stop working properly. Citrix says there is no evidence these issues have been used in real attacks, but these products have been targeted in the past for ransomware, so customers are urged to install the updates and make one recommended settings change for the HTTP/2 issue.

Summary
Citrix released patches for six NetScaler ADC and Gateway vulnerabilities that could allow arbitrary file reads, memory overreads, or denial-of-service under certain configurations. Customers should upgrade to fixed versions immediately and, for CVE-2026-13474, ensure the HTTP/2 small-window timeout setting is configured as required.

Citrix released security updates for six vulnerabilities in NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway that could be exploited to read arbitrary files or cause denial-of-service conditions. The flaws affect several configurations, including SAML IDP, Gateway or AAA virtual server, Oracle load balancing, DNS proxy or recursive resolver deployments, TCP Timestamp, and HTTP/2 profiles. Citrix said there is no evidence that the issues have been exploited in the wild.

The fixed versions include NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway 14.1-72.61 and later, 13.1-63.18 and later, NetScaler ADC 14.1-FIPS 14.1-72.61 FIPS and later, and NetScaler ADC 13.1-FIPS and 13.1-NDcPP 13.1.37.272 and later. For CVE-2026-13474, Citrix also advised customers to update the Http2SmallWndTimeout setting, because appliances not using HTTP Strict Profiles have a default value of 0 and upgrading alone will not completely address the vulnerability. Citrix credited Michael Tucker from the XOR team at JPMorgan Chase, Aliz Hammond of watchTowr, and Maxim Suhanov for reporting the vulnerabilities.

watchTowr Labs said CVE-2026-8451 was discovered while attempting to reproduce CVE-2026-3055, an earlier insufficient input validation flaw. The company said the issue comes from how NetScaler parses SAML authentication requests and can result in out-of-bounds memory reads when malformed SAML requests are sent. watchTowr noted that the newer issue leaks less data than CVE-2026-3055, but still raised concern about a broader pattern of fragile memory management in Citrix NetScaler appliances.

  • For CVE-2026-13474, upgrading alone may not fully fix the issue on appliances that are not using HTTP Strict Profiles, because customers must also manually set Http2SmallWndTimeout to 30 seconds.
  • watchTowr found CVE-2026-8451 while trying to reproduce CVE-2026-3055, and said the new SAML parsing issue shares the same root cause as the earlier flaw.
  • watchTowr reported that CVE-2026-8451 leaks less data than CVE-2026-3055, but varying the request length still allowed researchers to consistently extract a few bytes from the server.
  • The listed vulnerabilities are tied to specific NetScaler configurations, such as SAML IDP, Gateway or AAA virtual server, Oracle load balancing, DNS proxy or recursive resolver, TCP Timestamp, and HTTP/2 profiles.
  • The article emphasizes urgency because Citrix appliances have repeatedly been targeted in recent years, including by threat actors deploying ransomware.
Key Points
  • 01Citrix released security updates for six vulnerabilities in NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway that could enable arbitrary file reads or denial-of-service conditions.
  • 02The affected issues include CVE-2026-8451, CVE-2026-8452, CVE-2026-8655, CVE-2026-10816, CVE-2026-10817, and CVE-2026-13474, with CVSS scores ranging from 6.9 to 8.8.
  • 03Patches are available in NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway 14.1-72.61 and later, 13.1-63.18 and later, NetScaler ADC 14.1-FIPS 14.1-72.61 FIPS and later, and NetScaler ADC 13.1-FIPS and 13.1-NDcPP 13.1.37.272 and later.
  • 04For CVE-2026-13474, Citrix advised customers not using HTTP Strict Profiles to manually set Http2SmallWndTimeout to 30 seconds after upgrading, because the default value is 0.
  • 05Citrix said there is no evidence that the vulnerabilities have been exploited in the wild.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific vendor patch event affecting Citrix NetScaler ADC and Gateway, widely targeted edge products, with multiple high-severity flaws that can allow arbitrary file reads or denial-of-service. Although Citrix says there is no evidence of exploitation in the wild, the required upgrades and configuration change for CVE-2026-13474 make it important for security professionals to review.

Reports
  • PRIMARYNetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway Security Bulletin (CTX696604)advisory
    Citrix
    The article cites Citrix's security update bulletin as the source for the NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway vulnerabilities, affected versions, fixed releases, and mitigation guidance.
  • CitrixBleed to Infinity and Beyond: Citrix NetScaler Pre-Auth Memory Overread CVE-2026-8451research
    watchTowr Labs
    The article references watchTowr Labs' technical write-up explaining the SAML parsing issue behind CVE-2026-8451 and comparing it with CVE-2026-3055.
03research
Unpatched Argo CD Repo-Server Flaw Could Let Attackers Take Over Kubernetes Clusters
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 19:40 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSargo-cdargo-cd-repo-serverargo-cdownargoprojcve-2024-31989cve-2025-55190cve-2026-42880cycodegenerate-manifesthelmkuberneteskustomizeredisredis-cache-poisoningsynacktiv
88
In plain words

Researchers found an unfixed weakness in Argo CD, a widely used tool that helps organizations roll out software to Kubernetes, a system for running apps across many computers. If an attacker can reach one of Argo CD’s internal parts, they may be able to run their own code and eventually make the system deploy attacker-controlled software. This affects Argo CD users whose internal network protections are not enabled, especially some Helm chart installations where those protections are off by default. The real-world risk is serious because Argo CD can hold powerful access to software deployment systems and secrets, so a small opening could lead to control over a whole cluster.

Summary
Argo CD’s repo-server has an unpatched unauthenticated RCE flaw that can let an attacker with internal network access execute code and potentially take over Kubernetes clusters. Until a fix is available, users should enable strict Kubernetes network policies to isolate repo-server and Redis from other pods.

Argo CD has an unpatched flaw in its repo-server component that can let an unauthenticated attacker run code if they can reach the component’s internal network port. Synacktiv found the bug, reported it to Argo CD’s maintainers in January 2025, and says it remains unpatched roughly eighteen months later with no CVE. The firm says the issue can lead to a full Kubernetes cluster takeover.

The flaw affects repo-server, which reads Git repositories and builds Kubernetes manifests. Synacktiv says the internal gRPC service has no authentication, and a crafted GenerateManifest request can abuse kustomize’s –helm-command option to execute a script from an attacker-controlled Git repository instead of the helm binary. The attack was demonstrated against Argo CD v2.13.3, but the article says no full list of affected versions has been published.

The article says the main defense is network isolation because no patched version is available. Argo CD ships Kubernetes network policies that restrict repo-server access, but the Helm chart leaves them disabled by default with networkPolicy.create set to false. Synacktiv showed that code execution on repo-server could be used to read the Redis password, poison Argo CD’s cached deployment data, and cause Argo CD to deploy an attacker-supplied workload on the next automatic sync.

  • Argo CD includes Kubernetes network policy files that restrict repo-server access, but Helm-based installations do not enable them by default because networkPolicy.create is set to false.
  • The article says the earlier Redis cache-poisoning issue was only partially mitigated by adding a Redis password, because stealing that password from the repo-server can reopen the same path to attacker-controlled deployments.
  • Administrators can quickly check whether the main mitigation is active by running kubectl get networkpolicy -A and confirming that policies exist for components including repo-server and Redis.
  • Synacktiv is withholding its automated attack tool, argo-cdown, temporarily to give defenders time to enable network isolation before the tool is published.
Key Points
  • 01Synacktiv reported an unpatched flaw in Argo CD’s repo-server component that can let an unauthenticated attacker run code if they can reach the component’s internal network port.
  • 02The flaw abuses kustomize’s --helm-command option through the repo-server’s GenerateManifest service to execute a script from an attacker-controlled Git repository instead of the helm binary.
  • 03Synacktiv demonstrated the attack against Argo CD v2.13.3 and said there is no patched release and no CVE for the issue.
  • 04The Helm chart for Argo CD leaves Kubernetes network policies disabled by default, which can allow an attacker who compromises a single pod in the cluster to reach the repo-server and trigger the bug.
  • 05The article recommends enabling Kubernetes network policies so only Argo CD’s own components can reach the repo-server and Redis ports.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific, high-impact finding in widely used Kubernetes deployment software, with an unpatched unauthenticated repo-server code execution path that could lead to cluster takeover. There is no reported active exploitation or patch release, but the mitigation is immediately actionable through network isolation.

Reports
  • PRIMARYCaught in the Octopus Trap: Unauthenticated RCE in Argo CD with CodeQLresearch
    Synacktiv
    The article is based on Synacktiv’s research describing an unpatched unauthenticated remote code execution issue in Argo CD repo-server.
  • Revealing Argo CD Critical Vulnerabilityresearch
    Cycode
    The article references Cycode’s 2024 research on CVE-2024-31989, a Redis cache-poisoning issue in Argo CD.
  • CVE-2025-55190advisory
    Argo CD
    The article references this Argo CD advisory as a prior issue where a low-privilege API token could retrieve Git repository credentials.
  • CVE-2026-42880advisory
    Argo CD
    The article references this Argo CD advisory as a later issue that allowed read-only users to read plaintext Kubernetes secrets.
04research
Researcher Analyzes 3,000 Live ClickFix Payloads, Exposing API-Driven Malware Delivery
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 05:32 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSamsiapt28clearfakeclickfake-interviewclickfixdownloadfixfilefixirankimsukymuddywaternorth-koreapowershellrussiat1204-004windows-terminal
88
In plain words

A researcher studied about 3,000 live ClickFix tricks, where fake “prove you’re human” pages convince people to paste and run a harmful command themselves. He found that criminals now use back-end servers to give each visitor a freshly disguised version, and some pages hide the bad code in a downloaded file so it is harder to spot. This matters because ordinary computer users, Windows and macOS users, cryptocurrency workers, and organizations targeted by criminal or state-backed groups can all be affected, and the method is spreading quickly because it is cheap to change when defenders catch up.

Summary
ClickFix campaigns have evolved into API-driven malware delivery platforms that generate unique, obfuscated payloads for each visitor, making detection harder. Researchers also found a newer Downloads-folder method designed to bypass clipboard and script-scanning defenses, so defenders should focus on suspicious process chains rather than clipboard contents alone.

Security researcher Bert-Jan Pals analyzed roughly 3,000 payloads from live ClickFix campaigns and found that the technique has evolved into an API-driven malware delivery setup. ClickFix uses fake CAPTCHA or error pages to trick people into pasting and running commands that execute malware themselves. Pals found backend servers that check access tokens, log callers, and return freshly scrambled commands for each request.
