In one test, a server returned 100 different payloads for 100 requests, using rotating combinations of Base64, AES, TripleDES, Rijndael, and Deflate. After the wrapping was removed, the payloads unpacked to the same script, which ran in memory through a PowerShell runspace. The platform also serves lures in 25 languages, matches commands to the visitor’s operating system, and includes macOS versions alongside Windows versions.

The research also found a newer Downloads-folder method that quietly downloads a file and copies only a short command that moves, unpacks, and runs it, which is designed to avoid clipboard-focused detection and slip past Windows script scanning. The article says defenders should focus on process chains such as explorer.exe or WindowsTerminal.exe launching powershell.exe, cmd.exe, or msiexec.exe and making network connections soon afterward. Pals also listed comicstar[.]lat, babybon[.]cfd, and merkantalolol[.]asia as payload servers observed during the research, while noting that a connection to one of them does not prove infection.

  • ClickFix has moved beyond static lure pages into a commercialized, on-demand delivery model, making it cheap for attackers to keep changing payload disguises when defenders catch up.
  • Clipboard monitoring alone is no longer a reliable detection strategy because newer ClickFix pages can copy only a harmless-looking orchestrator command while keeping the malicious code in a downloaded file.
  • Windows Terminal use is notable for investigations because, unlike the older Windows+R Run-box method, it does not leave evidence in the RunMRU registry key that investigators normally check.
  • A connection to one of the listed payload servers indicates that a command was most likely placed in a user's clipboard, but the article warns it does not by itself prove infection.
  • ClickFix is no longer limited to ordinary cybercriminal campaigns, because Proofpoint tied Russia-, Iran-, and North Korea-backed groups including APT28, MuddyWater, and Kimsuky to its use.
Key Points
  • 01Security researcher Bert-Jan Pals analyzed roughly 3,000 payloads from live ClickFix campaigns and found that malicious commands are being delivered by API-driven backend servers.
  • 02One ClickFix server returned 100 different payload wrappers for 100 requests, using rotating combinations of Base64, AES, TripleDES, Rijndael, and Deflate while unpacking to the same script at the time of the research.
  • 03Newer ClickFix pages can quietly download a file to the Downloads folder and copy a short command that moves, unpacks, and runs the file, a method designed to avoid clipboard-focused detection and slip past Windows script scanning.
  • 04ClickFix lures are served in 25 languages, can match commands to a visitor’s operating system, and include macOS versions alongside Windows versions.
  • 05The article says defenders should focus on process chains such as explorer.exe or WindowsTerminal.exe launching powershell.exe, cmd.exe, or msiexec.exe and making network connections soon afterward.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a should-read technical finding on active ClickFix campaigns that are evolving into API-driven, per-visitor payload delivery and adding methods designed to bypass common detection points. It has broad relevance because the article cites large-scale growth, use by both criminals and state-backed groups, and concrete defensive signals and infrastructure observed in live campaigns.

Reports
  • PRIMARYClickFix: The Gift That Keeps on Givingresearch
    Bert-Jan Pals / KQLQuery
    The article is based primarily on Bert-Jan Pals' published analysis of roughly 3,000 live ClickFix payloads and several ClickFix platforms.
  • ESET Threat Report H1 2025research
    ESET
    The article cites ESET's report for its measurement of a 517% jump in ClickFix activity from late 2024 into the first half of 2025.
  • Microsoft Digital Defense Report 2025research
    Microsoft
    The article cites Microsoft's report for its finding that ClickFix accounted for 47% of initial-access cases seen by its Defender Experts team.
  • Around the World in 90 Days: State-Sponsored Actors Try ClickFixresearch
    Proofpoint
    The article references Proofpoint's analysis tying Russia-, Iran-, and North Korea-backed groups, including APT28, MuddyWater, and Kimsuky, to ClickFix campaigns.
05research
Phantom Squatting Uses AI-Hallucinated Domains for Phishing and Malware
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 07:20 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSandroidbangladeshbrand-impersonationlighthouselucidmontana-empirenpmpalo-alto-networks-unit-42phantom-squattingphantomravenslopsquattingtelegramunited-arab-emiratesusenix
86
In plain words

The article says AI tools sometimes invent web addresses, and criminals are starting to buy those made-up addresses and turn them into fake sites that steal information or push harmful apps. This affects people who trust links given by AI assistants, including users looking for banks, postal services, shopping sites, or betting sites. It matters because the fake sites can look real and may not be blocked right away, so people can be sent into danger by a tool they expected to help them.

Summary
Attackers are registering domains that AI models hallucinate and using them for phishing and malware, exploiting users’ trust in AI-generated links before reputation systems can flag the sites. Defenders can reduce risk by monitoring likely hallucinated domains and treating all AI-provided links as unverified until confirmed.

Palo Alto Networks’ Unit 42 says attackers are using a tactic it calls phantom squatting, where they register web domains that large language models invent and then host phishing pages or malware on them. The issue matters because people and AI assistants may treat model-provided links as trustworthy, even when the domain did not exist before. Whoever registers an invented domain first can benefit from that misplaced trust without needing a phishing email or malicious ad.

Unit 42 tested two AI models with 685,339 questions about 913 well-known brands and received 2.1 million links. Threat intelligence had already flagged 13,229 of those links as malicious, and roughly 250,000 invented domains had no owner. The article says newly registered phantom domains are hard for reputation systems to catch at first because they have no prior history of bad behavior.

The article describes two observed cases involving hallucinated postal-service domains that Unit 42 flagged before attackers registered them. In one case, an attacker registered a predicted domain 23 days later and used the Montana Empire phishing kit to steal card numbers, bank-transfer details, and national ID data; in another, an attacker registered a domain 51 days after it was flagged and used it to push a malicious Android app. The article advises people not to trust links just because AI provided them, to verify official domains, and to prevent AI agents from automatically opening or downloading from model-generated links without checks.

  • The article highlights that different AI models can invent the same fake domain for the same question, making future abuse predictable enough for defenders to monitor likely hallucinated domains before attackers register them.
  • Newly registered hallucinated domains are especially dangerous because they start with no reputation history, leaving blocklists and threat feeds with little to flag until after victims may already have been sent there.
  • The observed cases show that both attackers and defenders can reach the same fake domains by asking AI systems, turning model behavior itself into a way to identify likely phishing infrastructure in advance.
  • The risk increases when AI agents automatically open or download from model-generated links, because an agent may act on an invented address without the hesitation or verification a person might apply.
Key Points
  • 01Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 calls the practice of registering AI-hallucinated web domains for phishing and malware "phantom squatting" and says it is already occurring in the wild.
  • 02Unit 42 asked two AI models 685,339 questions about 913 well-known brands, receiving 2.1 million links, including 13,229 flagged as malicious and roughly 250,000 invented domains with no owner.
  • 03In one observed case, an attacker registered an AI-predicted fake domain resembling a national postal service's online marketplace 23 days after Unit 42 predicted it and used the Montana Empire phishing kit to steal card numbers, bank-transfer details, and national ID data.
  • 04In another observed case, Unit 42 flagged a hallucinated postal-service domain 51 days before an attacker registered it, cloned a brand site, added fake credibility claims, and used it to push a malicious Android app.
  • 05The article advises users not to trust links just because an AI provided them, to verify official domains before entering passwords, and to prevent AI agents from automatically opening or downloading from model-generated links without checks.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a concrete research finding with observed real-world phishing and malware cases, including domains predicted before attackers registered them. It is broadly relevant to security professionals because it shows how AI-generated links can become attacker-controlled infrastructure before normal reputation systems catch up.

Reports
  • PRIMARYPhantom Squatting: Hallucinated Web Domainsresearch
    Palo Alto Networks Unit 42
    The article is based on Unit 42 research that defines phantom squatting and reports evidence that attackers are registering AI-hallucinated domains for phishing and malware.
  • USENIX study on code-generating models suggesting nonexistent package namesresearch
    USENIX
    The article cites this study as evidence that code-generating models routinely suggest software package names that do not exist, connecting phantom squatting to slopsquatting.
06news
DHS confirms hackers breached HSIN info-sharing platform
bleepingcomputer.com · 01 Jul 2026 17:32 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSdepartment-of-homeland-securityfifa-world-cuphomeland-security-information-networkhsin-intelmicrosoft-sharepointoffice-of-intelligence-and-analysisunited-states
86
In plain words

Hackers broke into a Department of Homeland Security information-sharing system used by federal, state, local, international, and private-sector partners to share sensitive but unclassified information. DHS says it isolated the affected systems and found no sign that classified networks were impacted, but it is still unclear whether any documents were stolen. This matters because the system helps partners coordinate safety plans, respond to incidents, and share information about possible threats, including while the United States is handling security for World Cup games.

Summary
DHS confirmed hackers breached the unclassified Homeland Security Information Network, a sensitive information-sharing platform used by government and private-sector partners. The agency says it isolated affected systems and found no indication classified networks were impacted, but it remains unclear whether documents were stolen.

The Department of Homeland Security is investigating a cyberattack that compromised the Homeland Security Information Network, a sensitive but unclassified information-sharing platform. The intrusion was first reported by Nextgov and is believed to have occurred sometime between late May and early June, according to two people familiar with the matter. DHS has not attributed the attack to any specific threat actor or foreign government, and it remains unclear whether any documents were stolen.

Nextgov’s sources said the threat actors targeted HSIN servers as well as a SharePoint system used for collaboration. DHS said it immediately isolated the affected systems, mitigated the vulnerability, and launched a comprehensive forensic investigation. The department said there is no indication that classified networks were impacted and that the system remains operational for partners.

HSIN is used by federal, state, local, international, and private-sector partners to share sensitive but unclassified information, coordinate operations, manage incidents, and exchange information about potential threats. The article notes concern that the breach could have exposed security planning, interagency coordination, or response procedures while the United States is overseeing security for World Cup games. HSIN also had a previous security incident in 2023, when a contractor coding error exposed restricted HSIN-Intel data to all HSIN users by setting access permissions to “everyone.”

  • The affected environment was described by DHS as a “legacy” information-sharing system, indicating the incident involved an older unclassified platform that still remains operational for partners.
  • The reported targeting of both HSIN servers and a SharePoint collaboration system suggests the investigation is not limited to a single application but includes systems used for interagency collaboration.
  • The breach is especially sensitive because HSIN is used to coordinate safety, security, incident response, and information about potential threats during a period when the United States is overseeing security for World Cup games.
  • This is not HSIN’s first security issue, as a 2023 contractor coding error previously exposed restricted HSIN-Intel data to all HSIN users by setting access permissions to “everyone.”
Key Points
  • 01The Department of Homeland Security is investigating a cyberattack that compromised the Homeland Security Information Network, a sensitive but unclassified information-sharing platform.
  • 02The intrusion was carried out by an unknown threat actor in recent weeks and is believed to have occurred sometime between late May and early June, according to Nextgov sources.
  • 03DHS said it isolated the affected systems, mitigated the vulnerability, and launched a comprehensive forensic investigation.
  • 04DHS said there is no indication that classified networks were impacted and that the system remains operational for partners.
  • 05It remains unclear whether any documents were stolen from the system.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This reports a specific recent breach of DHS’s Homeland Security Information Network, a sensitive information-sharing platform used by government and private-sector partners. It is broadly relevant because the system supports incident coordination and security planning, though the article says classified networks were not impacted and data theft remains unclear.

07research
The SOC Files: ScreenConnect masked as freeware. An inside look at a large-scale campaign
securelist.com · 01 Jul 2026 10:00 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSasyncratconnectwisedll-sideloadingdns-jumpergermanykasperskymicrosoft-defenderobs-studiopowershellprocess-hollowingregasmscreenconnectseo-poisoningunited-statesvbscript
85
  • └
    news
    SEO-Poisoned Software Sites Abuse ScreenConnect to Deploy AsyncRAT
    thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 17:53 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
    TAGSasyncratbandicamdll-side-loadingdns-jumperds4windowskasperskymicrosoft-defenderobs-studiopowershellprocess-hollowingrussiascheduled-taskscreenconnectseo-poisoninguac
    In plain words

    Unknown attackers are creating fake download websites that look like pages for popular programs such as OBS Studio, DNS Jumper, DS4Windows, and Bandicam, and using search engines to make those sites easy to find. People and organizations who download from these sites can end up installing software that gives the attackers hidden remote control over Windows computers. This matters because the attackers can then steal sensitive information and watch what users are doing by recording the screen.

    Summary
    Attackers are using SEO-poisoned fake software download sites to trick users into installing ScreenConnect, which is then abused to deploy AsyncRAT. The campaign uses DLL side-loading, PowerShell scripts, Defender exclusions, and scheduled tasks to maintain control of infected Windows systems.

    Unknown threat actors are using the ScreenConnect remote access tool to deploy and execute AsyncRAT. Kaspersky described the activity as a massive, multi-domain, multi-language campaign that distributes malicious installer archives from spoofed websites. These websites impersonate popular software such as OBS Studio, DNS Jumper, DS4Windows, and Bandicam, and the attackers use search engine optimization to push them higher in Google and Bing results.

    The malicious archives contain a legitimate, signed Microsoft install.exe binary and a rogue install.res.1033.dll library, which is loaded through DLL side-loading to deploy the ScreenConnect service. Once ScreenConnect is running, it creates PowerShell and VBScript files that configure Microsoft Defender exclusions, disable UAC prompts, extract the AsyncRAT module, and run it using process hollowing. The malware connects to mora1987.work.gd, allowing the threat actor to control infected Windows systems, steal sensitive data, and monitor user activity by recording screen content.

    Persistence is established through a scheduled task named MasterPackager.Updater that runs script.vbs every two minutes and allows the attack to run again after reboot. Kaspersky said it identified more than 90 domain names localized across 10 languages, including English, Russian, Chinese, German, French, Spanish, Portuguese, and Arabic. Victims range from individual users to organizations.

    • The campaign lowers suspicion by bundling a legitimate, signed Microsoft install.exe binary with a malicious DLL, allowing the infection chain to start through software that appears trustworthy.
    • The attackers are not relying on a single fake site or language audience, as Kaspersky identified more than 90 localized domains across 10 languages.
    • The use of ScreenConnect gives the attackers remote control through a legitimate remote access tool before AsyncRAT is deployed, which can make the activity appear less obviously malicious at first.
    • The scheduled task runs every two minutes, showing that the attackers designed the infection to quickly restart after reboot or interruption.
    • The fake sites are promoted through search engine optimization, making ordinary searches on Google and Bing part of the delivery path for the malicious installers.
    Key Points
    • 01Unknown threat actors are leveraging the ScreenConnect remote access tool to deploy and execute AsyncRAT through a large multi-domain and multi-language campaign.
    • 02Kaspersky said the campaign distributes malicious installer archives from spoofed websites that impersonate popular software such as OBS Studio, DNS Jumper, DS4Windows, and Bandicam.
    • 03The malicious archives include a legitimate signed Microsoft install.exe binary and a rogue install.res.1033.dll library that is loaded through DLL side-loading to deploy the ScreenConnect service.
    • 04After ScreenConnect runs, it creates PowerShell and VBScript files that configure Microsoft Defender exclusions, disable UAC prompts, extract the AsyncRAT module, and run it using process hollowing.
    • 05The malware connects to the remote server mora1987.work.gd, and persistence is established through a scheduled task named MasterPackager.Updater that runs script.vbs every two minutes.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This reports a specific active malware distribution campaign abusing fake software sites, ScreenConnect, and AsyncRAT, with concrete technical details and indicators such as filenames, persistence mechanisms, and a remote server. It has broad relevance because it affects both individual users and organizations through common search-driven software downloads.

    Reports
    • PRIMARYThe SOC Files: ScreenConnect Campaign with AsyncRATresearch
      Kaspersky Securelist
      The article cites Kaspersky’s analysis as the source for details on the campaign’s malicious installers, DLL side-loading, ScreenConnect deployment, and AsyncRAT execution chain.
In plain words

Kaspersky found a large campaign where criminals made fake download websites for popular free programs like OBS Studio, DNS Jumper, DS4Windows, and Bandicam, and used search results to lead people to them. When someone installed the fake download, it quietly added ScreenConnect, a real remote access tool, and then used it to put harmful software on the computer so the attackers could keep control. The victims can be everyday people or organizations, and the broader risk is stolen credentials and unauthorized access that may later be sold on dark web marketplaces.

Summary
Kaspersky uncovered a large SEO-poisoning campaign that uses fake freeware download sites to install legitimate ScreenConnect remote access software via DLL sideloading, then deploy AsyncRAT for persistence and control. The key risk is that trusted remote administration tools can be silently abused at scale to compromise both consumers and corporate endpoints.

Kaspersky Managed Detection and Response investigated an incident in which the legitimate ScreenConnect remote access tool was used to deploy and execute AsyncRAT. The investigation expanded from a single case into a large campaign using malicious installer archives hosted on spoofed websites. Kaspersky uncovered more than 90 domain names localized across 10 languages, with fake sites impersonating software such as OBS Studio, DNS Jumper, DS4Windows, Bandicam, Glary Utilities, and Process Hacker.

In the initial incident, ScreenConnect spawned suspicious PowerShell and VBS scripts that changed Microsoft Defender exclusions, disabled User Account Control prompts, and launched a loader chain from the C:\Users\Public directory. The loader decrypted a PE binary from encoded data, reflectively loaded it, and used process hollowing to run AsyncRAT inside RegAsm.exe. Persistence was established through a scheduled task named MasterPackager.Updater that ran every two minutes, and the resulting RegAsm.exe process connected to the AsyncRAT C2 domain mora1987[.]work[.]gd.

Kaspersky traced the ScreenConnect installation to an archive downloaded from a typosquatted OBS Studio site, studioobs[.]com, which appeared in search engine results. The archive contained a legitimate Microsoft-signed install.exe, a malicious install.res.1033.dll loaded through DLL sideloading, and renamed MSI packages that installed both ScreenConnect and the impersonated software. The article states that the campaign affects both everyday consumers and corporate networks, and that its potential objective is credential theft and unauthorized access for later resale on dark web marketplaces.

  • The campaign’s use of a legitimate, signed Microsoft install.exe as a shared component gave investigators a reliable pivot for finding related fake installers across multiple software brands.
  • The attackers renamed MSI installer packages as .dll files, so defenders should not rely on file extensions alone when assessing installer archives.
  • The fake sites often appeared at the top of organic search results, showing that users could reach the malware through ordinary software searches rather than suspicious links or ads.
  • ScreenConnect activity became suspicious not simply because the tool was present, but because it ran as an Access or Support service with explicit server parameters and spawned tools such as PowerShell, schtasks, msiexec, and rundll32.
  • The loader chain deliberately excluded RegAsm.exe and broad disk paths from Microsoft Defender scanning before injecting AsyncRAT into RegAsm.exe, making Defender exclusion changes and unusual RegAsm.exe behavior especially important signals.
Key Points
  • 01Kaspersky MDR investigated an incident in which ScreenConnect was used to deploy and execute an AsyncRAT payload on a compromised system.
  • 02The investigation traced the ScreenConnect installation to a user-downloaded archive from a typosquatted OBS Studio website, studioobs.com, which appeared in search engine results.
  • 03The malicious archives contained a legitimate Microsoft-signed install.exe file and a rogue install.res.1033.dll library that was loaded through DLL sideloading to install ScreenConnect and the impersonated software.
  • 04After ScreenConnect ran, it created PowerShell and VBScript files that changed Microsoft Defender exclusions, disabled UAC prompts, loaded a decrypted PE file, injected AsyncRAT into RegAsm.exe, and created a scheduled task named MasterPackager.Updater for persistence.
  • 05Kaspersky identified more than 90 localized fake domains across 10 languages that impersonated popular software and used ScreenConnect command-and-control infrastructure to maintain access to compromised endpoints.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific, technically detailed investigation of a large-scale campaign abusing fake freeware sites, ScreenConnect, DLL sideloading, and AsyncRAT, with broad relevance to enterprise defenders and consumers. It includes concrete indicators, infrastructure, detection logic, and actionable behaviors to monitor, though it is not framed as a breaking critical exploitation event or a named threat actor operation.

08news
19-Year-Old Scattered Spider Suspect Extradited to Face U.S. Hacking Charges
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 19:28 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGS0ktapusdata-extortionestoniafinlandhelp-desk-impersonationinterpolmgm-resortsocto-tempestphishingscattered-spidersocial-engineeringunc3944united-kingdomunited-statesus-department-of-justice
82
  • └
    news
    Teen suspect in Scattered Spider hacks is extradited to US
    therecord.media · 01 Jul 2026 20:13 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
    TAGSdepartment-of-justiceestoniafbifinlandgoogle-voiceinterpolngroknorthern-district-of-illinoispeter-stokesscattered-spidersms-phishingsocial-engineeringunited-states
    In plain words

    A 19-year-old dual U.S. and Estonian citizen was brought from Finland to Chicago to face U.S. charges that he took part in Scattered Spider hacks. Officials say the group tricked a luxury jewelry retailer’s help desk into resetting employee logins, stole company data, and demanded \(8 million, leaving the company with about \)2 million in costs even though it did not pay. The case matters because the U.S. government says Scattered Spider has broken into more than 100 networks and collected more than $100 million in ransom payments.

    Summary
    A 19-year-old dual U.S.-Estonian citizen accused of being part of Scattered Spider was extradited from Finland to Chicago to face charges tied to alleged hacks, including an $8 million extortion attempt against a luxury jewelry retailer. The FBI says the group used social engineering to reset credentials, compromise privileged accounts, and cause about $2 million in losses.

    A 19-year-old man with dual U.S. and Estonian citizenship, Peter Stokes, was extradited from Finland to Chicago to face criminal charges tied to alleged participation in Scattered Spider hacks. The Department of Justice said he made an initial appearance Tuesday in federal court in the Northern District of Illinois. The FBI’s criminal complaint accuses him of conspiracy, cyber intrusion and fraud.

    The complaint centers on a May 2025 breach of an unspecified luxury-jewelry retailer referred to as Company F. The FBI alleges that Stokes and possibly other Scattered Spider members stole data from the company and demanded an \(8 million cryptocurrency ransom. The company did not pay, but the FBI said losses from business disruption, investigation and mitigation were about \)2 million, with further losses expected.

    According to the complaint, the suspects pretended to be company employees and asked the IT help desk to reset passwords and mobile devices used for multifactor authentication. They allegedly used Google Voice numbers for the calls, compromised three user accounts within about two to three hours, and accessed two IT administrator accounts with high privileges. The complaint also says the suspects used ngrok to maintain unauthorized access, and the U.S. government estimates Scattered Spider has been involved in more than 100 network intrusions and collected more than $100 million in ransom payments.

    • The jewelry retailer’s compromise escalated quickly because password and multifactor authentication resets through the help desk gave the suspects access to three accounts within about two to three hours, including two IT administrator accounts.
    • The suspects allegedly used ordinary services and tools, including Google Voice for help-desk calls and ngrok for persistent access, showing that legitimate technology was central to the alleged intrusion.
    • Even without paying the $8 million ransom, the victim company still reported about $2 million in losses from business disruption, investigation, and mitigation, with further losses expected.
    • The complaint links the same defendant not only to the 2025 jewelry retailer incident but also to unauthorized access at an online-communication platform in March 2023, indicating the charges cover activity spanning multiple years.
    Key Points
    • 01Peter Stokes, a 19-year-old with dual U.S. and Estonian citizenship, was extradited from Finland to Chicago to face charges related to alleged participation in Scattered Spider hacks.
    • 02The FBI’s complaint accuses Stokes of conspiracy, cyber intrusion and fraud, including a May 2025 breach of an unspecified luxury jewelry retailer referred to as Company F.
    • 03The FBI alleges that Stokes and possibly other Scattered Spider members stole data from the jewelry retailer and demanded an $8 million cryptocurrency ransom.
    • 04The complaint says the suspects used Google Voice numbers to call the company’s IT help desk, request credential resets, compromise three user accounts, and access high-privilege administrator accounts.
    • 05The U.S. government estimates that Scattered Spider has been involved in more than 100 network intrusions and collected more than $100 million in ransom payments.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This is a specific law enforcement development tied to Scattered Spider, a named threat actor associated with major intrusions and ransom activity. It is broadly relevant because the complaint details practical social-engineering methods used against help desks, including credential and multifactor authentication resets leading to privileged access.

In plain words

A 19-year-old accused member of Scattered Spider was sent from Finland to the United States to face charges that he helped break into companies, steal data, and demand large payments. The group has been tied to disruptions and theft affecting casinos, retailers, insurers, airlines, and other organizations, including well-known names like MGM Resorts, Caesars Entertainment, Marks & Spencer, Harrods, and Co-op. The case matters because police are increasingly identifying and arresting members of a once-hard-to-pin-down group that has caused major financial damage by tricking company help desks into giving them access.

Summary
A 19-year-old alleged Scattered Spider member has been extradited from Finland to the U.S. to face hacking, fraud, and extortion charges tied to multiple intrusions. The case highlights a widening law-enforcement crackdown on the social-engineering crew behind major attacks on casinos, retailers, insurers, and airlines.

Peter Stokes, a 19-year-old dual U.S. and Estonian citizen accused of belonging to Scattered Spider, has been extradited from Finland to face U.S. charges of conspiracy, computer intrusion, and fraud. The U.S. Department of Justice announced the extradition on July 1, and Stokes appeared in a Chicago federal court on June 30, where a judge ordered him held in custody. Finnish police arrested him in April on an Interpol Red Notice before his extradition in late June.

Court records identify Stokes by the online handle “Bouquet” and describe at least four intrusions, the first when he was 16. In one May 2025 case, prosecutors say he and others broke into a luxury jewelry retailer, copied its data, and demanded about \(8 million in cryptocurrency. The retailer refused to pay, removed the intruders, and spent at least \)2 million cleaning up, while Finnish officers seized two 2-terabyte hard drives when they stopped Stokes at Helsinki airport.

The article describes Scattered Spider as a loose, mostly English-speaking group of young people tracked under names including Octo Tempest, UNC3944, and 0ktapus. Its members often gain access by calling company IT help desks, pretending to be locked-out workers, and persuading staff to reset passwords or approve logins. The group has been linked to attacks on casinos, retailers, insurers, and airlines, and the article frames Stokes’s case as part of a wider law enforcement crackdown on members of the crew.

  • The article emphasizes that Scattered Spider’s most important weakness to exploit is often the help desk rather than the firewall, making stricter identity checks before password resets a practical defensive priority.
  • The group’s methods have continued to matter even after arrests, because Mandiant reported that other crews are already copying Scattered Spider’s playbook.
  • A joint advisory cited in the article warns that intruders may join a company’s internal chat tools and breach-response calls, allowing them to watch the people trying to remove them.
  • Devices seized from one alleged member, such as the two 2-terabyte hard drives taken from Stokes in Helsinki, may be valuable to investigators because they can lead to other members.
Key Points
  • 01Peter Stokes, a 19-year-old dual U.S. and Estonian citizen accused of belonging to Scattered Spider, was extradited from Finland to face U.S. charges of conspiracy, computer intrusion, and fraud.
  • 02Court records identify Stokes by the online handle "Bouquet" and describe at least four intrusions, including a May 2025 intrusion at a luxury jewelry retailer where attackers allegedly demanded about $8 million in cryptocurrency.
  • 03Scattered Spider is described as a loose, mostly English-speaking group of young people that uses help desk impersonation to gain access to company systems, steal files, and threaten leaks unless paid.
  • 04The group has been linked to major attacks on MGM Resorts and Caesars Entertainment in 2023 and later attacks on U.K. retailers, U.S. insurers, and airlines.
  • 05Recent arrests and guilty pleas involving alleged Scattered Spider members in the U.S. and U.K. show a broader law enforcement crackdown on the group.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This reports a specific law-enforcement action against an alleged member of Scattered Spider, a named threat actor tied to major intrusions across casinos, retailers, insurers, and airlines. It is broadly relevant because it includes concrete details on the group’s help-desk social engineering methods and the ongoing crackdown, though it is not an urgent patch or active-exploitation alert.

Reports
  • Scattered Spideradvisory
    CISA
    The article cites a joint U.S. and international advisory warning that Scattered Spider intruders may monitor company chat tools and join breach-response calls.

US lifts export controls on Anthropic’s frontier cybersecurity AI models
therecord.media · 01 Jul 2026 13:16 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSamazonanthropiccenter-for-ai-standards-and-innovationchinafable-5five-eyesgpt-5-5hackeronejailbreakkimi-k2-7mythos-5openaiproject-glasswingunited-statesus-commerce-department
82
  • └
    news
    Anthropic Restores Claude Fable 5 After U.S. Lifts Jailbreak-Linked Export Controls
    thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 06:46 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
    TAGSamazonanthropicchinaclaude-fable-5claude-mythos-5claude-opus-4-8googlegpt-5-5hackeronekimi-k2-7microsoftopenaiprompt-jailbreakunited-statesus-commerce-department
    In plain words

    Anthropic is bringing Claude Fable 5 back for users worldwide after the U.S. government lifted emergency limits that had forced the company to shut it off for everyone. The limits were triggered by a prompt trick that made the system ignore some safety rules and help show how software flaws could be abused. This matters because ordinary users, Anthropic staff, U.S. companies, and federal agencies were all affected, and because powerful AI tools can help people fix security problems but can also help others find ways to break into important systems.

    Summary
    Anthropic restored global access to Claude Fable 5 after the U.S. lifted emergency export controls triggered by a jailbreak concern, following new safety filters and government commitments. The episode highlights Washington’s still-improvised approach to regulating powerful dual-use AI models.

    Anthropic is restoring Claude Fable 5 worldwide on July 1 after the U.S. Commerce Department lifted export controls imposed about two and a half weeks earlier. The June 12 order had required Anthropic to cut off Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for any foreign national, including non-citizen staff inside or outside the United States. Because Anthropic said it could not reliably check every user’s nationality in real time, it shut both models down for everyone.

    The controls were triggered by a jailbreak found by Amazon researchers, which Anthropic says made Fable 5 flag some software flaws and, in one case, write code showing how a flaw could be abused. Anthropic trained a new classifier to detect the reported technique and says it now blocks it in more than 99% of attempts. Blocked requests are routed to the weaker Claude Opus 4.8, with users notified, though Anthropic says the change creates more false alarms for normal coding and debugging.

    Mythos 5 remains more restricted, with access restored June 26 for roughly 100 U.S. companies and federal agencies that defend critical infrastructure. Anthropic also agreed to look for security problems, coordinate with the government on future launches, report malicious use, and give the U.S. government earlier access to test future frontier models. The article says the episode shows that when Washington wants to move quickly on a frontier model, it still lacks a binding process and instead used improvised export controls.

    • The government used export controls as an emergency workaround because, according to the article, Washington still lacks a binding process for quickly regulating frontier AI model releases.
    • Anthropic’s mitigation does not simply block risky requests; it routes blocked Fable 5 requests to the weaker Claude Opus 4.8 and tells the user, accepting more false alarms on normal coding and debugging.
    • The same underlying model is being treated differently based on safety guardrails and user group, with Fable 5 restored worldwide while Mythos 5 remains limited to about 100 U.S. companies and federal agencies defending critical infrastructure.
    • Anthropic is pushing for jailbreaks to be ranked by practical harm, including capability gain, breadth, ease of weaponization, and discoverability, rather than treating every safety bypass as equally severe.
    • Competitive pressure shaped the policy debate, as the article says the pause came while cheap, capable Chinese open-source models were gaining ground and executives warned that freezing U.S. models gave rivals time to catch up.
    Key Points
    • 01Anthropic is restoring Claude Fable 5 worldwide on July 1 after the U.S. Commerce Department lifted export controls imposed on Fable 5 and Mythos 5 about two and a half weeks earlier.
    • 02The June 12 order required Anthropic to cut off both models for any foreign national, and the company shut both models down for everyone because it could not reliably verify every user’s nationality in real time.
    • 03The controls were triggered by a jailbreak found by Amazon researchers that Anthropic says made Fable 5 flag some software flaws and, in one case, write code showing how a flaw could be abused.
    • 04Anthropic trained a new classifier that it says blocks the reported jailbreak technique in more than 99% of attempts, while blocked requests are handed to Claude Opus 4.8 and users are told.
    • 05Mythos 5 access returned on June 26 for roughly 100 U.S. companies and federal agencies that defend critical infrastructure, while Anthropic continues working with the government to widen access.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This reports a specific, recent government action affecting access to major AI models after a jailbreak-linked security concern, with implications for AI safety controls, vulnerability research, and critical infrastructure users. It is broadly relevant to security professionals, though it is not an active exploitation campaign, breach, or urgent patch advisory.

  • └
    news
    Anthropic to restore Claude Fable access on Wednesday
    bleepingcomputer.com · 01 Jul 2026 00:35 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
    TAGSanthropicclaudeclaude-fable-5claude-mythos-5department-of-commerceunited-states
    In plain words

    Anthropic says the U.S. Department of Commerce has lifted restrictions on its two most powerful Claude models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, and the company will start bringing back access to Fable 5 on Wednesday. People who use Claude are affected, especially those waiting to regain access, but Mythos 5 will still be limited to select companies. It is not yet clear whether Fable 5 will be available to everyone outside the United States, and mentions of identity checks on Anthropic’s website have raised concerns that access could be limited at first.

    Summary
    Anthropic says the U.S. Department of Commerce has lifted export controls on Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5, and it will begin restoring Fable 5 access on Wednesday. Mythos 5 will remain limited to select companies, and it is still unclear whether Fable 5 access will be restored globally.

    Anthropic confirmed that the Department of Commerce has lifted export controls on Claude’s two most powerful models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5. In a post on X, the company said it will begin restoring access to Fable 5 on Wednesday. Anthropic said it will share another update soon and thanked users for their patience, as well as those who worked on redeploying the models.

    Mythos 5 will remain exclusive to select companies, even after the export controls were lifted. The article says it is unclear whether Fable 5 will roll out to everyone, including users outside the United States. It also notes that references to KYC were recently spotted on Anthropic’s website, raising concerns that models like Fable could be restricted to users in the United States, at least initially.

    • The lifting of export controls does not mean full availability for both models, because Anthropic says Mythos 5 will still remain limited to select companies.
    • Access restoration may be phased or regionally limited, since the article says it is unclear whether Fable 5 will be available outside the United States.
    • References to KYC on Anthropic’s website suggest that identity verification could become part of access to models like Fable 5, according to concerns noted in the article.
    Key Points
    • 01Anthropic confirmed that the Department of Commerce has lifted export controls on Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5.
    • 02Anthropic said it will begin restoring access to Claude Fable 5 on Wednesday.
    • 03Claude Mythos 5 will remain exclusive to select companies.
    • 04Anthropic said it is grateful to users for their patience and to those who worked on redeploying the models.
    • 05The article says it is unclear whether Fable 5 will roll out to everyone, including users outside the United States.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This is a specific, recent event involving Anthropic, the U.S. Department of Commerce, and access to Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5, but it does not report an active threat, breach, vulnerability, or patch. Security professionals may find it relevant for AI access and policy awareness, but it is low urgency from an operational security standpoint.

In plain words

The U.S. government has lifted restrictions that had forced Anthropic to shut off worldwide access to its Fable 5 security-focused AI model, after the company agreed to new safety checks and closer government testing. The shutdown affected Anthropic’s customers and raised concern among more than 100 security professionals, who warned that taking powerful tools away from defenders could help adversaries catch up. The broader issue is how the U.S. will control advanced AI tools that can be useful for protecting computer systems but may also be misused.

Summary
The U.S. lifted export controls on Anthropic’s Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity AI models after Anthropic agreed to new safeguards, government pre-release testing, and jailbreak reporting measures. The reversal may shape how frontier AI cybersecurity tools are regulated going forward.

Anthropic restored global access to its Fable 5 model after the U.S. government lifted export controls that had barred foreign nationals from accessing the advanced cybersecurity-focused AI tool. The restrictions had led Anthropic to disable access for all customers for roughly three weeks to ensure compliance. Export controls on the more powerful Mythos 5 model were also fully lifted as of June 30, though access remains limited to vetted U.S. organizations through Project Glasswing.

The episode was significant because it marked the first known use of export control authorities to remove AI software, rather than chips or hardware, from public access. Anthropic said the initial shutdown was triggered by a jailbreak technique covered in an Amazon research report, but Katie Moussouris of Luta Security argued that the described process was normal defensive work: finding, fixing, and testing code. Anthropic also said its testing found the same technique worked against other models, including OpenAI’s GPT-5.5 and Kimi K2.7, which did not face comparable restrictions.

As part of the agreement to restore Fable 5 access, Anthropic trained a new safety classifier that it says blocks the specific technique in more than 99% of cases. The company also committed to expanded pre-release government evaluation, rapid disclosure of significant jailbreaks, joint research resources, participation in a voluntary security standard, and a HackerOne bug bounty program for cyber jailbreak submissions. More than 100 cybersecurity professionals had signed an open letter warning that the controls could harm defenders while adversaries’ models continue to advance.

  • The reversal may set a precedent for regulating frontier AI models in the U.S. because the original action was the first known use of export control authorities to remove AI software, rather than chips or hardware, from public access.
  • The disputed “jailbreak” centered on using AI to find, fix, and test vulnerable code, which Katie Moussouris argued is also one of the model’s core defensive uses and cannot be removed without reducing its value to legitimate security work.
  • Anthropic’s testing weakened the case for model-specific restrictions by finding that the same technique worked against OpenAI’s GPT-5.5 and Kimi K2.7, which did not face comparable export controls.
  • The agreement points toward a regulatory model based on pre-release government testing, rapid jailbreak disclosure, bug bounties, and shared severity scoring rather than a broad shutdown of model access.
  • The open letter’s concern was not just about Anthropic’s access but about competitive defensive capability, arguing that Chinese open-weight models are only months behind leading U.S. models.
Key Points
  • 01Anthropic restored global access to its Fable 5 model after the U.S. government lifted export controls that had forced a roughly three-week shutdown.
  • 02Export controls on Anthropic’s Mythos 5 model were also fully lifted as of June 30, but access remains limited to vetted U.S. organizations through Project Glasswing.
  • 03Anthropic said the initial shutdown was triggered by a jailbreak technique from an Amazon research report, but later testing found the same technique worked against other models including OpenAI’s GPT-5.5 and Kimi K2.7.
  • 04As part of the agreement to restore access, Anthropic trained a new safety classifier that it says blocks the specific technique in more than 99% of cases and committed to expanded government evaluation and rapid jailbreak disclosure.
  • 05More than 100 cybersecurity professionals signed an open letter warning that the export controls could do more harm than good by removing advanced defensive capabilities while adversaries’ models continue to improve.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This reports a specific and unusual government action affecting access to frontier cybersecurity AI models, along with its reversal and new safeguards. It is broadly relevant to security professionals because it may shape how advanced defensive AI tools are regulated, though it is not an active exploitation event, breach, or critical patch.

Reports
  • The Fable 5 Export Controls Harm US Cyber Defenseopinion
    Luta Security
    The article cites Katie Moussouris of Luta Security as describing in detail the technique that triggered the initial shutdown and arguing it was not a guardrail bypass.
10research
AI-Generated Browser Ransomware Abuses Chromium API on Windows and Android
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 12:59 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSandroidcheck-point-researchchinachromiumcve-2023-4863deepseekdiscordfile-system-access-apigoogle-chromein-browser-ransomwareinfernograbberisraelvirustotalwindowswinlocker
82
  • └
    research
    Browser-Only Ransomware: From LLM Hallucinations to a Practical Attack Technique
    research.checkpoint.com · 01 Jul 2026 10:05 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
    TAGSandroidbrowser-only-ransomwarecheck-point-researchchromiumdeepseekdiscordfile-system-access-apifirefoxgoogle-chromeinfernograbberiossafarishowdirectorypickershowopenfilepickerwebview
    In plain words

    Check Point Research found that an AI-generated malicious web page could point attackers toward a real Chrome feature that lets a website read and change files after the user approves a folder access prompt. In a test, this could be used as a fake AI photo-enhancement site that tricks Android users into giving access to photo folders, then locks or steals those pictures without installing an app or using a browser flaw. This matters because people may treat a normal-looking website prompt as harmless, while their personal photos, documents, and other sensitive images could be exposed.

    Summary
    Check Point researchers found that LLMs can turn vague malicious prompts into practical browser-only ransomware concepts by abusing Chrome’s File System Access API, letting a malicious website encrypt or steal user-selected files after a legitimate-looking permission prompt. The risk is especially concerning on Android, where fake AI photo tools could trick users into granting access to valuable photo folders without any app install or browser exploit.

    Check Point Research analyzed a DeepSeek-attributed Python Flask sample that served a victim-facing web page disguised as a Discord avatar AI upscaler. The sample attempted to combine browser-based stealing, surveillance, file access, and ransomware-style behavior, but most of its claimed capabilities failed at normal browser security boundaries. The important exception was its use of the File System Access API, which pointed to a real browser capability that can let a web page read, enumerate, and modify user-selected files after permission is granted.

    The researchers describe this as “In-Browser Ransomware,” a browser-only technique that does not require an APK installation, native payload, browser exploit, or root access. The attack relies on social engineering and a legitimate browser permission prompt, using a fake utility to persuade the victim to grant access to a local folder. The article says this is significant because the AI-generated sample connected unrealistic malware ideas with a practical browser feature, showing how LLMs can lower the expertise needed to operationalize attack paths.

    The Android scenario is highlighted as especially concerning because modern Chrome versions expose the picker-based File System Access API to web content, while iOS browsers do not expose the same primitives. In the researchers’ controlled proof of concept, a fake AI image upscaler could ask the user to choose a photo directory and then encrypt pictures during a fake processing step. The article recommends treating browser folder-access prompts as high-stakes decisions, avoiding granting websites access to sensitive directories, keeping backups, and being skeptical of unfamiliar AI-branded tools.

    • The article highlights that an AI-generated malware sample can be mostly nonfunctional yet still reveal a practical abuse path by connecting unrealistic attacker goals to a real browser feature.
    • The Android risk is amplified because a fake photo-enhancement workflow gives users a plausible reason to approve folder-level access to DCIM or picture directories, making the permission prompt seem consistent with the advertised service.
    • The research found that direct ransomware prompts were refused by DeepSeek V4, but removing explicit wording while preserving the same file-access and destructive behavior led to working browser-based proof-of-concept code in some cases.
    • The article notes that Chromium blocks or constrains access to many sensitive system locations, but selection of the root Pictures and Videos directories was not restricted in the researchers’ Android and Windows testing.
    • The practical defensive takeaway is that browser folder-access prompts should be treated as high-stakes security decisions, not routine website interactions.
    Key Points
    • 01Check Point Research analyzed a DeepSeek-attributed Flask sample that attempted to create a browser-based stealer and ransomware tool, but most of its claimed capabilities failed at normal browser security boundaries.
    • 02The sample’s important working idea was to use the File System Access API, including showOpenFilePicker() and showDirectoryPicker(), to let a web page read, enumerate, and potentially modify files after the user grants access.
    • 03The researchers built a controlled proof of concept showing that a fake AI image upscaler could use Chrome on Android to access selected photo directories and encrypt pictures during a fake processing step.
    • 04The technique does not require installing an APK, exploiting a browser vulnerability, deploying a native payload, or obtaining root access; it relies on social engineering and a legitimate browser permission prompt.
    • 05The article says the Android scenario is especially concerning because Chrome exposes the File System Access API and mobile photo directories often contain highly sensitive personal data.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This is a specific research finding with broad relevance: Check Point analyzed a DeepSeek-attributed sample and demonstrated a practical browser-only ransomware workflow using Chrome’s File System Access API, especially affecting Android photo directories. It is not reported as active exploitation in the wild, but the technique is novel, concrete, and actionable for security professionals.

    Reports
    • OpenAI’s cyber-safety documentationguide
      OpenAI
      The article cites OpenAI’s cyber-safety documentation as describing safeguards for models classified as having High Cybersecurity Capability.
    • Detecting and countering cyber misuse of Clauderesearch
      Anthropic
      The article references Anthropic’s published reports on detecting and countering cyber misuse of Claude while discussing AI vendor cyber-safety controls.
    • PRIMARYFile System Access specificationresearch
      WICG
      The article cites the File System Access specification because it explicitly lists ransomware as a security consideration for the API.
    • PRIMARYRoB: Ransomware over Modern Web Browsersresearch
      USENIX Security
      The article cites this 2023 USENIX Security paper as prior research that studied abuse of the File System Access API to encrypt local files from a malicious web application.
In plain words

Researchers at Check Point found a harmful web app, apparently generated with DeepSeek, that can trick people using Chrome or other Chromium-based browsers on Windows or Android into giving a website access to a folder, then copy, lock, and overwrite files and show a ransom demand. The fake site posed as a Discord avatar upscaler and also included features to steal Discord tokens, payment details, crypto recovery phrases, keystrokes, and webcam or microphone feeds. There is no evidence this browser-only file-locking method has been used in real attacks yet, but it matters because it shows AI tools may help less-skilled attackers turn broad harmful ideas into working attacks using normal browser features.

Summary
Check Point researchers found AI-generated malware dubbed InfernoGrabber that uses Chromium’s File System Access API to run browser-only ransomware on Windows and Android after tricking users into granting folder access. The key concern is that LLMs like DeepSeek may lower the skill needed to discover and build novel attack chains using legitimate browser features.

Check Point researchers flagged a malware artifact generated using DeepSeek that created a working browser-only ransomware technique for Windows and Android devices. The sample is a Python Flask application named “deepseek_python_20260125_da0631.py,” uploaded to VirusTotal on January 25, 2026, and described there as a fully functional information stealer and ransomware toolkit. The malware author named it InfernoGrabber v9.0, and the application is designed to operate as a malicious web server using a fake Discord avatar AI upscaler as a lure.

The article says the technique tricks a user into granting file system access to a web page, after which the page can enumerate files in the selected folder, read and exfiltrate their contents, encrypt and overwrite them, and display an extortion note. Check Point emphasized that this can occur without installing a native payload, exploiting a browser vulnerability, or requiring root access. The approach is limited to browsers exposing the picker-based File System Access API, including Google Chrome and other Chromium-based browsers on Windows and Android, and there is no evidence that this browser-native ransomware pattern has been abused in the wild.

The broader significance is that Check Point says DeepSeek helped bridge a gap between a theoretical browser-only ransomware risk and a practical attack chain. The article states that DeepSeek’s models have lower refusal rates for malicious cyber requests compared with Western counterparts from Anthropic, Google, or OpenAI, and that the sample could be generated from a single broad prompt. Check Point argues this shows AI can lower the expertise needed to produce offensive code and may surface unusual attack techniques by combining legitimate platform features in unexpected ways.

  • The article highlights that the ransomware path does not depend on a browser flaw; it abuses a legitimate permission prompt, making user consent to file access the critical security decision.
  • Check Point’s analysis suggests that a broad malicious prompt can be enough for an AI model to surface an attack technique the requester may not know exists, shifting discovery of novel methods away from only skilled human attackers.
  • DeepSeek is presented as a particular concern because the article says it has lower refusal rates for malicious cyber requests than Western models and can generate a working malicious application from a single broad prompt.
  • The technique is especially notable because it affects both Windows and Android through Chromium-based browsers, extending the concern beyond traditional desktop ransomware scenarios.
  • The article says there is no evidence this browser-native ransomware pattern has been used in the wild, which makes the finding an early warning rather than a report of an active campaign.
Key Points
  • 01Check Point researchers flagged a malware artifact generated using DeepSeek that combines unrealistic browser-malware concepts with a real browser capability to create a working ransomware technique running entirely inside the browser on Windows and Android devices.
  • 02The sample, named InfernoGrabber v9.0 by its author, is a Python Flask application uploaded to VirusTotal on January 25, 2026 and described as a fully functional information stealer and ransomware toolkit.
  • 03The malicious web server lures victims with a fake Discord avatar AI upscaler while including functions to steal Discord tokens, harvest credit card numbers and cryptocurrency seed phrases, log keystrokes, capture webcam and microphone feeds, and exfiltrate data through a hard-coded Discord webhook.
  • 04The browser-only ransomware technique tricks users into granting file system access to a web page, then enumerates files in the selected folder, reads and exfiltrates their contents, encrypts and overwrites them, and displays an extortion note without installing a native payload, exploiting a browser vulnerability, or requiring root access.
  • 05Check Point said the approach is limited to browsers exposing the picker-based File System Access API, including Google Chrome and other Chromium-based browsers on Windows and Android, and there is no evidence the browser-native ransomware pattern has been abused in the wild.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific research finding about an AI-generated malware artifact demonstrating a novel browser-only ransomware technique affecting Chromium-based browsers on Windows and Android. It is broadly relevant to security professionals because it shows how legitimate browser permission features can be abused without a native payload or browser exploit, though the article states there is no evidence of in-the-wild abuse.

Reports
  • PRIMARYBrowser-Only Ransomware: From LLM Hallucinations to a Practical Attack Techniqueresearch
    Check Point Research
    The article cites Check Point’s research as the basis for the findings about AI-generated browser-only ransomware abusing Chromium’s File System Access API.
11news
New ChocoPoC malware targets researchers via trojanized PoC exploits
bleepingcomputer.com · 01 Jul 2026 20:08 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSchocopoccompiled-native-python-extensioncve-2025-14847cve-2025-55182cve-2025-64446cve-2026-0257frintgithublogcrypt-cryptographymapboxpypisekoiaskytextslogsectrojanized-poc-exploits
82
In plain words

Security researchers found that several GitHub projects claiming to contain example exploit code were secretly installing a spying program called ChocoPoC through hidden Python add-ons. The people most affected are cybersecurity researchers, vulnerability testers, and penetration testers who download and run this kind of code while studying security flaws. Once installed, the program can run commands, steal browser data and files, collect system details, and send information out through online services. This matters because even code that appears useful for security work can be turned into a trap, so untrusted projects should only be tested in isolated environments.

Summary
Attackers are targeting cybersecurity researchers with GitHub proof-of-concept exploits that look legitimate but install malicious PyPI dependencies delivering the ChocoPoC RAT. Researchers should treat unverified PoCs as hostile and run them only in isolated environments.

Multiple weaponized proof-of-concept exploits on GitHub were found delivering a Python-based remote access trojan named ChocoPoC. The campaign is believed to target cybersecurity researchers who download and test PoC exploits. Sekoia identified at least seven GitHub repositories distributing ChocoPoC through exploits for vulnerabilities affecting FortiWeb, React2Shell, MongoBleed, PAN-OS, Ivanti Sentry, Check Point VPN, and Joomla SP Page Builder.

ChocoPoC stands out because the malware is not embedded directly in the exploit file. Instead, malicious Python packages are added to the PoC dependency list, with a trojanized package named frint pulling a malicious dependency named skytext from PyPI. When the PoC runs, a compiled native Python extension decrypts embedded Python code, triggers a downloader, and retrieves the final ChocoPoC payload from a Mapbox dataset.

The RAT can execute shell commands and Python code, upload files and directories, collect browser passwords, cookies, autofill data, and browsing history, search for documents and database files, gather shell history and network configuration, and enumerate running processes. Sekoia found that skytext was downloaded 2,400 times, mostly on Linux-based systems, with download surges after disclosure of a popular vulnerability. The researchers assessed with high confidence that the attacker primarily used compromised accounts to publish malicious PyPI packages and PoC repositories, and they recommend running unverified code only in isolated environments.

  • The campaign’s delivery method is harder to spot because the exploit code can remain intact while the malicious behavior is shifted into seemingly harmless dependency packages.
  • The attackers used PyPI as part of the infection chain, meaning reviewing only the GitHub repository code may not reveal the malware risk.
  • Mapbox datasets were used both to retrieve the final ChocoPoC payload and to exfiltrate data, showing that legitimate online services can be abused in multiple stages of the same campaign.
  • Download spikes after disclosure of a popular vulnerability show that newly publicized flaws can quickly become lures for researchers seeking proof-of-concept code.
  • Sekoia’s findings indicate that compromised accounts were likely used to publish the malicious packages and repositories, reducing the reliability of judging a project by account identity alone.
Key Points
  • 01Multiple weaponized proof-of-concept exploits on GitHub were found delivering a Python-based remote access trojan named ChocoPoC in a campaign believed to target cybersecurity researchers.
  • 02ChocoPoC is delivered through malicious Python packages added to the proof-of-concept dependency list, rather than by embedding the malware directly in the exploit file.
  • 03Sekoia identified at least seven GitHub proof-of-concept repositories distributing ChocoPoC for vulnerabilities affecting FortiWeb, React2Shell, MongoBleed, PAN-OS, Ivanti Sentry, Check Point VPN, and Joomla SP Page Builder.
  • 04The skytext package used in the campaign was downloaded 2,400 times, mostly on Linux-based systems, with download spikes following disclosure of a popular vulnerability.
  • 05Sekoia assessed with high confidence that the attacker primarily used compromised accounts to publish malicious PyPI packages and proof-of-concept repositories.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific finding about active malicious GitHub proof-of-concept repositories and PyPI packages delivering the ChocoPoC RAT to security researchers. It is broadly relevant because researchers and testers commonly run PoC code, and the campaign uses a notable dependency-based delivery method that may evade simple repository review.

Reports
  • PRIMARYDon’t eat the ChocoPOCs: How vulnerability researchers were repeatedly targeted by trojanised exploitsresearch
    Sekoia
    The article cites Sekoia’s research as the source for findings on the ChocoPoC campaign, malicious PyPI packages, GitHub repositories, download counts, and attacker account usage.

VEIL#DROP Malware Chain Uses Blogger Platform to Deliver PureLogs Stealer
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 17:18 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSaspnet-compilerbloggerblogspotdrive-by-compromisefileless-executioninstallutilmsbuildpowershellpurelogs-stealerreflective-code-loadingregsvcsspearphishingveil-dropwindows-script-hostxor-obfuscation
82
In plain words

Researchers found a new online scam that tricks people into opening a file that looks like a PDF, while secretly using Google’s Blogger service to load data-stealing software called PureLogs. People whose computers are infected can have sensitive information stolen, and that stolen data can then help attackers move further into a company’s systems or cloud services. This matters because the attackers hide their activity behind trusted services and normal-looking programs, making the theft harder to notice.

Summary
Researchers uncovered a multi-stage malware chain called VEIL#DROP that uses deceptive JavaScript files, PowerShell, and Blogger-hosted payloads to secretly deliver the PureLogs information stealer. The campaign abuses trusted services and living-off-the-land binaries to evade detection and reduce forensic traces.

Cybersecurity researchers have identified a new multi-stage malware delivery chain called VEIL#DROP that uses social engineering and Blogger pages to deliver the PureLogs information stealer. Securonix suspects the initial payloads are distributed through spear-phishing or drive-by compromise. The chain begins with a JavaScript file disguised as a document, such as transcript.pdf.js, which runs through Windows Script Host and launches PowerShell with execution policy bypasses enabled.

The PowerShell script retrieves a next-stage payload from a Blogger page, allowing attackers to abuse Google’s trusted infrastructure as a staging mechanism and blend in with legitimate web activity. The loader can display a benign page like Google to make it appear that a PDF document opened while the infection continues in the background. It also attempts to allow unrestricted PowerShell execution, terminate selected processes such as wscript.exe, delete the original JavaScript file, and decrypt an embedded payload.

Securonix said the loader uses XOR decryption, dynamic Blogspot URL construction, runtime mutation, in-memory execution, and reflective .NET loading to reduce detection and forensic artifacts. If direct in-memory execution is blocked, it can fall back to Microsoft-signed binaries including regsvcs.exe, installutil.exe, msbuild.exe, and aspnet_compiler.exe. The article notes that PureLogs Stealer can harvest sensitive data from compromised hosts, and that stolen data may help attackers move deeper into an environment, establish persistence, perform lateral movement, or breach cloud infrastructure.

  • The use of Blogger as a staging point lets the attackers blend malicious traffic with ordinary access to Google-owned infrastructure, making reputation-based blocking less reliable.
  • The loader changes its Blogspot URL format by inserting random forward slashes, so defenses that rely on exact URL matches or static indicators may miss related executions.
  • The malware does not depend on a single trusted Windows binary for fallback execution; it tries multiple Microsoft-signed tools in sequence, increasing the chance that at least one method will work.
  • The fake document behavior includes opening a benign page such as Google, which can make victims believe the file opened normally while the infection continues in the background.
  • The article highlights that PureLogs infections can have consequences beyond one computer because stolen data may be used to deepen access into the victim environment, including cloud infrastructure.
Key Points
  • 01Securonix identified a new multi-stage malware delivery chain called VEIL#DROP that uses social engineering and Blogger pages to deliver the PureLogs information stealer.
  • 02The infection chain begins with a JavaScript file disguised as a document, such as transcript.pdf.js, which runs through Windows Script Host and launches PowerShell with execution policy bypasses enabled.
  • 03The PowerShell script retrieves a next-stage payload from a Blogger-hosted page, allowing the attackers to abuse Google’s trusted infrastructure as a staging mechanism.
  • 04The loader uses techniques including XOR decryption, dynamic Blogspot URL construction, runtime mutation, in-memory execution, and reflective .NET loading to evade detection and reduce disk artifacts.
  • 05If direct in-memory execution is blocked, the loader can fall back to Microsoft-signed binaries such as regsvcs.exe, installutil.exe, msbuild.exe, and aspnet_compiler.exe to execute the recovered .NET assemblies.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific malware-delivery finding involving VEIL#DROP and PureLogs Stealer, with concrete techniques such as Blogger-hosted staging, PowerShell loading, fileless execution, and LOLBin abuse. It is broadly relevant for defenders because it shows how attackers are using trusted infrastructure and runtime mutation to evade common detection approaches, though the article does not describe a major breach or critical widespread exploitation event.

Reports
  • PRIMARYVEIL#DROP: Blogspot-Hosted PowerShell Loaderresearch
    Securonix
    The article cites Securonix researchers' analysis as the source for the VEIL#DROP malware chain that uses Blogger-hosted payloads to deliver PureLogs Stealer.

Ousaban Banking Trojan Targets Iberian Bank Users with Fake PDF Lures
thehackernews.com · 01 Jul 2026 15:26 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSbanco-santanderbrazilcasbaneiroclickfixfortinetgeofencinggrandoreiroguildmajavalimelcozousabanportugalspainsteganographytetrade
82
In plain words

A Brazilian banking program called Ousaban is being used to target Windows users in Spain and Portugal through fake PDF files that pretend something is broken and ask the person to press “Update.” If installed, it waits until the person opens a banking website, then can record what they type, take screenshots, change copied text, show fake messages, and let the attacker control the computer. This matters because it can help criminals take over real bank accounts at banks such as Banco Santander, BBVA, CaixaBank, Bankinter, and Caixa Geral de Depósitos.

Summary
Ousaban, a Brazilian banking trojan, is targeting Windows users in Spain and Portugal with fake corrupted-PDF lures that geofence victims and hide the payload inside an image. Once installed, it waits for banking sessions to steal credentials and enable account takeover.

A Brazilian banking trojan called Ousaban is targeting Windows users who bank in Spain and Portugal, according to a May 2026 campaign identified by Fortinet’s FortiGuard Labs. The campaign begins with a phishing PDF disguised as a corrupted file that prompts the victim to press an “Atualizar” button. The goal is to steal banking logins and take over accounts.

Ousaban waits quietly on an infected Windows PC until the user opens a targeted banking site. When a target bank loads, it can capture screenshots and keystrokes, tamper with the clipboard, show fake messages, and give the attacker remote control. The malware watches for more than two dozen banks across Spain and Portugal, including Banco Santander, BBVA, CaixaBank, Bankinter, and Caixa Geral de Depósitos.

The delivery chain screens visitors so that users outside Spain or Portugal receive a Spanish “access denied” notice instead of malware. If the visitor passes the check, a script downloads an image that looks like a PDF icon but hides a ZIP file containing Ousaban, then runs the malware and deletes the image, ZIP, and script. Fortinet says defenders should watch for a registry Run key named Financeiro and files dropped to C:\SysMain\_5874288.

  • Automated sandbox checks may miss the campaign because the operators moved visitor screening to the server side, meaning a sandbox that only fetches the link may see only an "access denied" page instead of the malware.
  • Blocking a single command server address is unlikely to be effective because Ousaban generates a new real server location each day using the current date from a Google page and a fixed secret.
  • The Pastebin-based server address embedded in Ousaban is a decoy, so defenders should not assume that the visible command server reference is the real control infrastructure.
  • The persistence artifact is unusually specific and actionable: Fortinet says defenders should watch for a Windows registry Run key named "Financeiro" and files dropped under C:\SysMain\_5874288.
  • The campaign combines older Ousaban behavior with a newer delivery wrapper that uses geofencing, a hidden payload inside an image, and throwaway daily infrastructure to limit exposure to non-victims and security tools.
Key Points
  • 01Fortinet's FortiGuard Labs identified a May 2026 campaign in which the Brazilian banking trojan Ousaban targets Windows users who bank in Spain and Portugal.
  • 02The attack begins with a phishing PDF disguised as a corrupted file that prompts the victim to press an "Atualizar" button, which opens a malicious webpage.
  • 03The campaign screens visitors so that users outside Spain or Portugal receive a Spanish "access denied" notice instead of the malware.
  • 04After passing the screening, a script downloads an image that looks like a PDF icon but hides a ZIP file containing Ousaban, then runs the malware and deletes evidence of the download.
  • 05Once installed, Ousaban waits for the user to open targeted banking sites and can capture screenshots and keystrokes, tamper with the clipboard, show fake messages, and give attackers remote control.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

The article reports a specific active Ousaban banking trojan campaign targeting Windows banking users in Spain and Portugal, with concrete delivery methods, victim geography, affected banks, and defender indicators. It is especially relevant for security teams handling email security, endpoint detection, or financial-sector threats, though its scope is geographically focused rather than global.

Reports
  • PRIMARYAnalysis of Ongoing Ousaban Attacks Targeting the Iberian Peninsularesearch
    Fortinet FortiGuard Labs
    The article says Fortinet's FortiGuard Labs identified the May 2026 Ousaban campaign and later cites Fortinet's findings on screening, infrastructure, indicators, and detections.
  • The Tetrade: Brazilian banking malwareresearch
    Kaspersky Securelist
    The article references Kaspersky's earlier labeling of Ousaban/Javali, Grandoreiro, Guildma, and Melcoz as the "Tetrade" of Brazilian banking trojans.
