Healthcare’s Governance Needs To Speed Up To Combat Unsanctioned AI Usage
health-isac.org · 01 Jul 2026 17:04 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSelectronic-health-reportererrol-weisshealth-isac
45
In plain words

The article says hospitals and other health sector organizations are struggling to formally adopt AI, while employees may already be using these tools in unofficial ways. This affects healthcare organizations because their rules and oversight are not keeping up with how AI is being used inside and outside their walls. The broader concern is that this gap creates risks for organizations that already face technical and regulatory hurdles.

Summary
Health sector organizations are seeing growing unsanctioned use of AI as formal governance and adoption processes lag behind employee demand. The key takeaway is that healthcare leaders need faster, more practical AI governance to reduce security, privacy, and compliance risks from shadow AI.

The article argues that healthcare governance needs to move faster to address unsanctioned AI usage. It says hospitals have spent the last decade modernizing their digital infrastructure. Despite that modernization, formal adoption of new tools has been difficult because of technical and regulatory hurdles and, at times, resistance from employees.

The article says there is a widening gap between how health sector organizations want to use AI and how AI is actually being used inside and outside their walls. It frames the issue around an unofficial AI ecosystem, the risks of shadow AI, and the need for governance to evolve. It also points to closing the gap and a broader shift that is underway.

The piece is by Errol Weiss, chief security officer at Health ISAC, and directs readers to the full article in Electronic Health Record. Its significance is that healthcare organizations are facing AI use that may be happening outside formal processes while governance struggles to keep pace. The article presents this as a health-sector issue tied to digital modernization, regulation, employee behavior, and organizational oversight.

  • Digital modernization in hospitals has not been enough to make formal adoption of new tools easy, because technical hurdles, regulatory hurdles, and employee resistance remain barriers.
  • The article frames unsanctioned AI as both an internal and external issue for health sector organizations, not only a problem of tools used by employees inside hospital walls.
  • The article’s section themes indicate that addressing shadow AI requires governance changes and gap-closing efforts, not only warnings about risk.
Key Points
  • 01The article argues that healthcare governance needs to speed up to address unsanctioned AI usage.
  • 02Hospitals have spent the last decade modernizing their digital infrastructure.
  • 03Formal adoption of new tools in hospitals has been difficult because of technical and regulatory hurdles and, at times, employee resistance.
  • 04The article says there is a widening gap between how health sector organizations want to use AI and how AI is actually being used inside and outside their walls.
  • 05The article frames unsanctioned AI use as an issue involving an unofficial AI ecosystem, shadow AI risks, evolving governance, and efforts to close the gap.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is an opinion/trend piece about healthcare governance lagging behind unsanctioned AI use, not a report on a specific incident, vulnerability, breach, or active threat. It may be relevant for healthcare security leaders, but under the scoring rules generic governance and best-practice commentary is low priority.

06news
Japanese insurer, brewer, manufacturer and telecom disclose cyber breaches
therecord.media · 01 Jul 2026 13:10 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGS8baseaflac-life-insurance-japanblackfield-ransomwarecanadaeverestjapankddinidecpokkasapporo-holdingssingaporesleeman-breweriestaiwanunited-statesvietnam
40
In plain words

Several large Japanese companies reported separate computer break-ins or suspected break-ins, including insurer Aflac Japan, beverage company Sapporo’s overseas subsidiaries, manufacturer Nidec’s Taiwanese subsidiary, and telecom company KDDI. Aflac Japan said information for about 4.38 million policyholders was exposed, including names, addresses and phone numbers, and some customers also had payment account information affected. KDDI warned that customers’ email addresses and passwords may have been obtained, while Sapporo and Nidec are still investigating whether data was stolen. These incidents matter because they can expose people’s personal information, interrupt company operations, and show how attacks on major businesses can affect customers across several countries.

Summary
Several major Japanese companies, including Aflac Japan, Sapporo subsidiaries, Nidec and KDDI, disclosed separate cyber incidents involving exposed customer data, disrupted systems and ongoing investigations. The largest known breach affected Aflac Japan, where personal data for about 4.38 million policyholders was exposed.

Several major Japanese companies disclosed cyber incidents over the past two weeks that exposed customer data, disrupted internal systems, and prompted investigations. The affected companies include Aflac Life Insurance Japan, Sapporo Holdings subsidiaries, Nidec’s Taiwanese subsidiary, and KDDI Corporation. The article says there is no evidence so far that the attacks are connected or were carried out by the same threat actor.

Aflac Japan reported the largest disclosed breach, saying hackers compromised its customer portal and other systems and exposed personal information for about 4.38 million policyholders. The exposed data includes names, addresses, and phone numbers, while about 230,000 customers also had premium payment account information compromised. Aflac said it suspended parts of its systems after detecting the intrusion but continued handling claims and customer inquiries through call centers and other channels.

Sapporo Holdings disclosed suspected unauthorized access affecting Singapore-based Pokka and Canadian brewer Sleeman Breweries, and said it shut down affected systems while investigating whether data was stolen. Nidec disclosed a ransomware breach affecting part of the network at its Taiwanese subsidiary, Nidec Chaun Choung Technology, and the BlackField ransomware group later claimed it stole more than two terabytes of corporate data. KDDI said attackers exploited a vulnerability in third-party software to gain unauthorized access to an email system it operates for five Japanese internet service providers, and warned that customers’ email addresses and passwords may have been obtained.

  • Several companies emphasized limits on the incidents’ reach: Aflac said its U.S. business was unaffected, Sapporo said it found no impact on domestic operations, and Nidec said the affected subsidiary used an independent network.
  • KDDI’s incident affected five separate Japanese internet service providers through an email system it operates for them, showing that one provider’s software issue can expose customers across multiple companies.
  • Aflac suspended parts of its systems after detecting the intrusion but continued handling insurance claims and customer inquiries through call centers and other channels.
  • Nidec has now disclosed ransomware-related incidents involving overseas manufacturing subsidiaries in both Taiwan and Vietnam, with the earlier 2024 incident claimed by the 8Base and Everest ransomware groups.
Key Points
  • 01Several major Japanese companies disclosed cyber incidents over the past two weeks, and the article says there is no evidence so far that the attacks are connected or were carried out by the same threat actor.
  • 02Aflac Life Insurance Japan said hackers compromised its customer portal and other systems, exposing personal information for about 4.38 million policyholders, including names, addresses and phone numbers.
  • 03Aflac Japan said about 230,000 customers also had their premium payment account information compromised, while the incident was limited to its Japanese operations and did not affect its U.S. business.
  • 04Sapporo Holdings disclosed suspected unauthorized access affecting Singapore-based Pokka and Canadian brewer Sleeman Breweries, and said it shut down affected systems while investigating whether any data was stolen.
  • 05KDDI Corporation said attackers exploited a vulnerability in third-party software to access an email system it operates for five Japanese internet service providers, and warned that customer email addresses and passwords may have been obtained.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a roundup of multiple apparently unrelated cyber incidents affecting Japanese companies rather than coverage of a single event. Per the scoring guidance, aggregator or summary pieces should be scored exactly 40.

07announcement
Monthly Newsletter – July 2026
health-isac.org · 01 Jul 2026 13:22 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSaamianthropicarizonaclaude-mythoscretefdagreecehealth-isacliving-securityquest-diagnosticsridge-securityunited-statesva-oig
40
In plain words

This Health-ISAC newsletter announces upcoming healthcare security events, including summits in Crete and Tucson, plus workshops, webinars, and new reports. It highlights research with Quest Diagnostics about Claude Mythos, an AI tool described as able to find and use software weaknesses on its own, which could make serious online attacks easier for non-experts. Healthcare organizations, their vendors, partners, and people who rely on healthcare services are affected because these risks could threaten critical health infrastructure as such tools spread.

Summary
Health-ISAC’s July 2026 newsletter highlights upcoming European and Americas Summit deadlines, new workshops and webinars, and several new healthcare cybersecurity resources, including research on Claude Mythos and guidance on third-party IAM. The key takeaway is that Health-ISAC is emphasizing preparation for emerging AI-driven cyber risks while promoting upcoming community events and security resources.

Health-ISAC’s July 2026 newsletter highlights upcoming events, recent regional workshop activity, and new resources for the healthcare security community. The 2026 Health-ISAC European Summit, “Security Cove,” will take place October 12–16, 2026, in Crete, Greece, with early bird registration and subsidized hotel accommodations. The newsletter also promotes a Friday post-Summit excursion to Knossos Palace with an expert guide and a seaside lunch.

The newsletter announces that the 2026 Health-ISAC Fall Americas Summit, “Orbit of Trust,” will take place November 30–December 4, 2026, in Tucson, Arizona. Abstract submissions close on July 10, and summit registration opens on September 9 with a one-day member sale offering registration for US$99. It also points readers to upcoming workshops and webinars, including an on-demand webinar by Ridge Security on new vulnerabilities aimed at the healthcare industry.

A major resource highlighted is Health-ISAC and Quest Diagnostics’ joint research on Claude Mythos, an Anthropic AI model described as having autonomous zero-day vulnerability discovery and weaponization capabilities. The newsletter says Mythos outperforms previous models in conventional and offensive security benchmarks and enables non-experts to execute complex exploits independently. It also highlights Health-ISAC’s white paper on managing third-party identity and access management, a Human Risk Management Maturity Model report by Living Security, and other related healthcare cybersecurity resources.

  • Health-ISAC is framing autonomous AI vulnerability discovery as an urgent health-sector risk by stating that tools like Claude Mythos are projected to proliferate globally by late 2026 and could let non-experts carry out complex exploits.
  • Healthcare organizations with vendor or partner access relationships have a specific new resource to act on, as Health-ISAC’s third-party IAM white paper breaks down challenges, best practices, and real-world use cases for managing those identities.
  • Members interested in the Fall Americas Summit have a narrow cost-saving window, because the newsletter says registration opens with a one-day US$99 member sale on September 9.
  • The European Summit includes direct cost reductions for lodging, with Health-ISAC subsidizing single-occupancy rooms by €50 per night and double-occupancy rooms by €75.
Key Points
  • 01The July 2026 Health-ISAC newsletter highlights the 2026 Health-ISAC European Summit, scheduled for October 12–16, 2026, in Crete, Greece, with early bird registration and subsidized hotel accommodations.
  • 02The newsletter announces a Friday post-Summit excursion to Knossos Palace with an expert guide and a seaside lunch featuring Cretan cuisine.
  • 03The 2026 Health-ISAC Fall Americas Summit, titled “Orbit of Trust,” is scheduled for November 30–December 4, 2026, in Tucson, Arizona, with abstract submissions closing on July 10 and registration opening on September 9.
  • 04Health-ISAC and Quest Diagnostics conducted joint research on Claude Mythos, an Anthropic AI model described as having autonomous zero-day vulnerability discovery and weaponization capabilities.
  • 05The newsletter points to new resources including a Health-ISAC white paper on managing third-party IAM, a Human Risk Management Maturity Model report by Living Security, and an on-demand webinar by Ridge Security on new vulnerabilities aimed at the healthcare industry.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a monthly newsletter that aggregates multiple unrelated items, including event announcements, workshop photos, webinars, and links to resources. It does not report a single specific incident, active exploitation, breach, or urgent patch, so it is low priority under the digest/summary rule.

Reports
  • Human Risk Management Maturity Modelresearch
    Living Security
    The newsletter describes this as a report by Living Security, a Health-ISAC Champion in the Community Services program.
08advisory
Microsoft fixes GIF functionality in the Windows Emoji Panel
bleepingcomputer.com · 01 Jul 2026 10:52 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSbitlockergiphygooglekb5095093microsoftmicrosoft-update-catalogtenorwindows-11windows-11-23h2windows-11-24h2windows-11-25h2windows-11-26h1windows-emoji-panelwindows-server-2025
35
In plain words

Microsoft fixed a problem that made GIFs disappear from the Windows 11 Emoji Panel after the old GIF provider, Tenor, shut down the service Windows had been using. People using Windows 11 24H2, 25H2, or 26H1 can get the fix by installing the KB5095093 update, which switches the feature to GIPHY. People on Windows 11 23H2 and Windows Server 2025 are still affected while Microsoft works on a fix. This matters because a small outside service change broke a built-in Windows feature, showing how everyday computer functions can depend on third-party providers.

Summary
Microsoft fixed the broken GIF search in the Windows 11 Emoji Panel for some versions by switching from Tenor to GIPHY in the optional KB5095093 update. The issue still affects Windows 11 23H2 and Windows Server 2025 while Microsoft works on a fix.

Microsoft has fixed GIF functionality in the Windows Emoji Panel for Windows 11 users after the previous provider shut down its service. The feature stopped working suddenly on June 30 for some users because Google’s Tenor GIF search engine retired its API. Affected devices could show a message saying “GIF service is not available” instead of GIF options.

Microsoft restored the feature for Windows 11 24H2, 25H2, and 26H1 users by switching to GIPHY in the preview KB5095093 Windows cumulative update released on June 23. The company said users should install the latest available Windows updates to restore GIF functionality. Microsoft is still working on a fix for Windows 11 23H2 and Windows Server 2025 and has not provided a timeline.

KB5095093 can be installed through the Microsoft Update Catalog or by checking for updates in Windows Update. Because it is an optional update, users may need to click “Download and install” unless they have enabled “Get the latest updates as soon as they’re available.” The update also includes the Point-in-Time Restore feature and fixes a Recycle Bin confirmation dialog bug that displayed internal file names instead of normal file names.

  • The fix for Windows 11 24H2, 25H2, and 26H1 is in an optional preview update, so some users will not receive it unless they manually choose "Download and install" or have the "Get the latest updates as soon as they're available" setting enabled.
  • Microsoft’s replacement of Tenor with GIPHY shows that the Windows Emoji Panel’s GIF feature depends on an external GIF provider rather than being fully controlled within Windows itself.
  • The same known issue remains unresolved on Windows 11 23H2 and Windows Server 2025, so installing the latest available update only restores GIF functionality for the newer Windows 11 releases listed by Microsoft.
  • KB5095093 bundles the Emoji Panel fix with unrelated operating system changes, including Point-in-Time Restore and a Recycle Bin dialog fix, so users installing it receive more than just the GIF provider change.
Key Points
  • 01Microsoft fixed GIF functionality in the Windows Emoji Panel for Windows 11 users after Google's Tenor GIF search engine retired its API on June 30, 2026.
  • 02The issue caused some devices to show a message saying "GIF service is not available" instead of GIF options in the Windows Emoji Panel.
  • 03Microsoft restored the feature for Windows 11 24H2, 25H2, and 26H1 users by switching to GIPHY in the preview KB5095093 Windows cumulative update released on June 23.
  • 04Microsoft is still working on a fix for Windows 11 23H2 and Windows Server 2025 and has not provided a timeline.
  • 05The optional KB5095093 update also includes Point-in-Time Restore and fixes a Recycle Bin confirmation dialog bug involving internal file names.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific Windows update item, but it concerns GIF functionality in the Emoji Panel rather than a security vulnerability, exploitation, breach, or threat actor activity. Security professionals may only care indirectly because it relates to Windows update behavior and affected versions.

Reports
  • PRIMARYKB5095093 Windows cumulative updateadvisory
    Microsoft
    The article cites Microsoft’s KB5095093 preview cumulative update as the update that switches the Windows Emoji Panel GIF provider to GIPHY and restores GIF functionality for some Windows 11 versions.
09sponsored
Turning Indicators into Intelligence in OpenCTI with Criminal IP
bleepingcomputer.com · 01 Jul 2026 14:01 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGScriminal-ipcriminal-ip-connectoropenctitor
35
In plain words

The article describes a sponsored integration between Criminal IP and OpenCTI that adds extra context to suspicious internet addresses, website names, and links, such as risk scores, ownership details, location, known weaknesses, and phishing signs. It matters because security teams using OpenCTI can more quickly understand which online clues are most dangerous, how they may be connected, and where attackers may be using related infrastructure. This can help analysts investigate phishing pages, exposed services, and suspicious networks faster and make better decisions about what to prioritize.

Summary
Criminal IP’s OpenCTI integration enriches IPs, domains, and URLs with reputation scores, infrastructure context, vulnerability links, behavioral signals, and phishing analysis. The goal is to turn raw indicators into connected OpenCTI graph intelligence that helps analysts investigate, correlate, and prioritize threats faster.

The article describes the Criminal IP integration with OpenCTI, which enriches IP addresses, domains, and URLs with contextual threat intelligence. It adds reputation scoring, infrastructure intelligence, vulnerability data, behavioral signals, and phishing analysis to indicators ingested into OpenCTI. The enriched information is structured as OpenCTI entities and relationships so analysts can investigate connected infrastructure and prioritize higher-risk indicators.

A central feature is Criminal IP’s dual-perspective risk scoring, which reflects both how an IP is targeted and how it behaves externally. The integration also creates relationships involving CVEs, Autonomous Systems, geolocation, observed services, and resolved IP infrastructure. For domains, Criminal IP performs full URL analysis to identify phishing activity, credential harvesting, suspicious files, impersonation techniques, and phishing probability.

The article presents SOC triage, alert validation, threat hunting, infrastructure pivoting, and phishing campaign analysis as key use cases. Analysts can use the enriched graph data to pivot across indicators, network ownership, geographic context, and exposed services. The article is sponsored and written by Criminal IP, and it promotes using the connector to turn isolated indicators into structured intelligence within OpenCTI.

  • The integration’s value is not just enrichment, but converting isolated indicators into connected graph data that analysts can pivot across for relationships such as shared infrastructure, network ownership, location, and vulnerabilities.
  • Criminal IP’s separate inbound and outbound risk scores give analysts a way to distinguish between infrastructure that is being targeted and infrastructure that is behaving suspiciously toward others.
  • The article emphasizes that an indicator can be prioritized not only because it appears malicious, but because it is linked to exposed services or known CVEs that may make it exploitable.
  • Domain risk is presented as quantifiable through phishing probability, with confidence scores tied directly to findings such as credential harvesting, suspicious files, and impersonation techniques.
Key Points
  • 01The Criminal IP integration with OpenCTI enriches IP addresses, domains, and URLs with reputation scoring, infrastructure intelligence, vulnerability data, behavioral signals, and phishing analysis.
  • 02Criminal IP provides dual-perspective risk scoring for IP addresses, covering both inbound targeting and outbound behavior.
  • 03The integration structures enriched data as OpenCTI entities and relationships, including CVEs, Autonomous Systems, geolocation, and resolved IP infrastructure.
  • 04For domains, Criminal IP performs full URL analysis to detect phishing activity, credential harvesting, suspicious files, and impersonation techniques.
  • 05The article describes SOC triage, threat hunting, infrastructure pivoting, and phishing campaign analysis as key use cases for the integration.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is sponsored product-focused content describing the Criminal IP integration with OpenCTI rather than a specific incident, vulnerability, active exploitation, or major security finding. It may be useful for teams evaluating those tools, but it is low priority for most security professionals.

This matters because web applications depend on many separate pieces, and if one weak piece is left in place, the whole application can be put at risk. The people affected are anyone responsible for a web application and anyone who relies on that application working safely.

Summary
The article emphasizes that outdated or vulnerable web application components are a major security risk and should be continuously inventoried, monitored, and patched. Organizations should use dependency scanning and vulnerability databases to identify risky libraries or frameworks and replace or update them promptly.

The article is titled “Finding and Addressing Vulnerable and Outdated Web Application Components.” The provided article body is not readable as normal prose and appears to contain corrupted or nonsensical characters. 11announcement
Webinar: Why traditional email security is no longer enough
bleepingcomputer.com · 01 Jul 2026 16:54 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSabnormal-aiaccount-takeoverbleepingcomputerbusiness-email-compromisedevice-code-phishingnovant-health
35
In plain words

BleepingComputer is hosting a live webinar on July 8, 2026 about why older email security tools may miss newer scams that look like normal business messages. The article says attackers increasingly pretend to be trusted coworkers, vendors, or business partners, which can affect organizations and their employees. This matters because security teams may spend a long time figuring out whether a message is dangerous or an account has been taken over, while automated tools that study normal behavior may help them respond faster.

Summary
BleepingComputer is hosting a July 8, 2026 webinar on how behavioral AI can help detect and automate response to modern phishing, BEC, and account takeover attacks that bypass traditional email security by abusing trusted identities and workflows.

BleepingComputer will host a live webinar on July 8, 2026 at 2 PM ET titled “Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI.” The session will be presented by Dan Nickolaisen, Solutions Architect Manager at Abnormal AI, and Eric Danneker, Director of Cyber Vigilance and Defense at Novant Health. The webinar focuses on why traditional email security approaches such as secure email gateways, reputation services, and signature-based detection are no longer enough on their own.

The article says modern phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks increasingly bypass traditional defenses by exploiting trusted identities and legitimate business workflows. Many of these attacks do not rely on malicious attachments, known malware, or suspicious domains. Instead, attackers impersonate trusted colleagues, vendors, and business partners while abusing legitimate authentication workflows to blend into normal business communications.

The webinar will examine how behavioral AI can help security teams automate detection, investigation, and remediation. Abnormal AI applies behavioral AI to analyze communication patterns and account activity, with the goal of identifying suspicious behavior, reducing manual investigations, and accelerating response workflows. Attendees are expected to learn practical approaches for identifying sophisticated email threats that traditional controls may overlook and for improving operational efficiency through automation.

  • Organizations cannot rely only on indicators such as malicious attachments, known malware, or suspicious domains because the article says many current attacks avoid those signals entirely.
  • Trusted business relationships have become part of the attack surface, since attackers increasingly impersonate colleagues, vendors, and business partners to make malicious emails appear normal.
  • Device Code phishing is highlighted as a specific technique that can circumvent traditional detection methods, making authentication workflows a focus for email security review.
  • The article frames investigation workload as a major operational problem, not just a detection problem, because teams must determine whether an email is malicious, whether an account is compromised, and what containment actions are needed.
Key Points
  • 01BleepingComputer will host a live webinar on July 8, 2026 titled "Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI."
  • 02The webinar will examine how modern phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks bypass traditional email defenses.
  • 03The article says many current email attacks impersonate trusted colleagues, vendors, and business partners rather than relying on malicious attachments, known malware, or suspicious domains.
  • 04The webinar will discuss how behavioral AI can help automate detection, investigation, and remediation workflows for email threats.
  • 05Speakers include Dan Nickolaisen, Solutions Architect Manager at Abnormal AI, and Eric Danneker, Director of Cyber Vigilance and Defense at Novant Health.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is primarily a webinar announcement about behavioral AI for email security, not a report on a specific incident, vulnerability, breach, or active campaign. While the topic is relevant, it is promotional and discusses broad trends rather than urgent, concrete findings.

12commercial
What Is Agentic Runtime Security?
mitiga.io · 01 Jul 2026 17:39 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSagentic-runtime-securityappomnicrowdstrikehelios-aidribmmandiantmitigaoauthotter-ai
35
In plain words

The article explains Mitiga’s new approach called Agentic Runtime Security, which is meant to spot and stop attacks happening across cloud services, workplace apps, sign-in systems, AI tools, and outside services, not just laptops and servers. It says this matters because attackers now use stolen logins, approved app connections, chatbots, copilots, and other tools to move very quickly through business systems, often in seconds or minutes. The people most affected are security teams, company leaders, and organizations that depend on these online services, because a missed attack can spread across many systems and cause costly damage before anyone notices.

Summary
Mitiga argues that traditional EDR is no longer enough because modern attacks move through cloud, SaaS, identity, AI agents, and third-party services where endpoint tools have little visibility. Its Agentic Runtime Security offering is positioned as log-based runtime detection and response for stopping active attacks across that broader infrastructure before business impact.

Mitiga introduced Agentic Runtime Security for modern infrastructure, describing it as runtime detection and response across cloud, SaaS, identity, AI, and third-party services. The article argues that EDR protects endpoints and workloads, while Mitiga’s approach protects the areas where endpoint tools cannot see. It says the modern business environment now depends on services, cloud platforms, SaaS applications, identity providers, AI, and third-party connections rather than only devices and servers.

The article emphasizes that attackers are moving faster than many defenses can respond, citing a 22-second median handoff time for initial access in 2025 and a 29-minute average eCrime breakout time. It contrasts those figures with a median dwell time of 14 days and an average breach identification and containment time of 241 days. Mitiga argues that this gap cannot be closed by watching endpoints harder because attacks now cross cloud, SaaS, identity, AI, and third-party services.

Technically, Agentic Runtime Security is presented as log-based rather than agent-based, using the audit trails emitted by each platform where there is no workload to instrument. Mitiga says its Cloud Security Data Lake provides long-horizon, forensic-grade context across cloud, SaaS, identity, AI, and third-party services to reconstruct and stop attacks while they are happening. The article also highlights coverage for shadow SaaS, AI-era SaaS risks such as meeting transcription tools, and non-human identities including chatbots, copilots, autonomous agents, OAuth apps, and API tokens.

  • The article highlights a major timing mismatch: attackers may move or hand off access in seconds, while defenders still often identify and contain breaches over days or months.
  • Mitiga argues that many organizations can believe they have adequate visibility through posture tools, prevention controls, compliance dashboards, and SIEM logs, yet still lack the cross-domain awareness needed to catch active misuse in real time.
  • The article treats unsanctioned AI-enabled SaaS differently from older shadow SaaS because a single meeting transcription tool can expose conversations from many people who never installed or approved the tool.
  • The article emphasizes that non-human identities such as chatbots, copilots, OAuth apps, and API tokens are now important security subjects because they can act with their own credentials and permissions inside business systems.
  • Mitiga’s examples suggest that endpoint-based tools may miss unauthorized SaaS activity because that activity can be driven by identity and cloud services rather than by signals on a user’s device.
Key Points
  • 01Mitiga describes Agentic Runtime Security as runtime detection and response across cloud, SaaS, identity, AI, and third-party services that anticipates, detects, interrupts, and stops active attacks before they reach the business.
  • 02The article argues that EDR protects endpoints and workloads, while Agentic Runtime Security uses platform audit logs to protect areas where endpoint agents cannot operate.
  • 03Mitiga says modern attackers move quickly across cloud, SaaS, identity, AI, and third-party services, citing figures such as a 22-second median handoff time for initial access in 2025 and a 29-minute average eCrime breakout time.
  • 04The article states that Mitiga’s approach depends on a forensic-grade, long-horizon data layer because cloud and service environments do not provide disk images or memory dumps like endpoints do.
  • 05Mitiga positions its Helios AIDR model around three pillars: Defend with AI, Defend from AI, and Defend the AI, all built on an AI-native foundation.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is primarily a vendor product-marketing article and broad trend discussion about runtime security across cloud, SaaS, identity, and AI. It does not report a specific active attack, breach, vulnerability, patch, or named threat actor operation, so it is low priority for most security professionals.

13opinion
Martin Lee: Running through the Arctic (and the threat landscape)
blog.talosintelligence.com · 01 Jul 2026 10:00 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGScisco-talosemeahumans-of-talosmartin-lee
25
In plain words

The article is an interview with Martin Lee, Cisco Talos’s EMEA Lead, about how he moved from studying human viruses to working on the early internet and then into online security after taking a job writing spam filters. It matters because his story shows how today’s security work grew out of real problems people and organizations began facing online, including early hidden intrusions that were not well understood at the time. The broader message is that people starting or changing careers can help protect others by staying curious, visible, and open to unexpected opportunities.

Summary
Cisco Talos profiles Martin Lee, EMEA Lead, tracing his accidental path from human virus genetics to early internet work and then cybersecurity through spam filtering and early APT detection. His main advice is to stay curious, visible, and open to unexpected career pivots.

This article is a Humans of Talos interview with Martin Lee, EMEA Lead at Talos. Lee describes his current job as largely ambassadorial, involving research into changes in the threat landscape and explaining those changes to customers and partners. The article presents his role as a bridge between threat research and the people who need to understand what is important and different.

Lee explains that he began his career researching the genetics of human viruses before discovering the internet very early on. He left academia because he believed the internet would become the biggest invention of his lifetime and wanted to be part of building it. He worked on the early internet and building websites during its rapid growth in the mid to late 1990s.

After the dot-com crash, Lee took a job writing spam filters at a time before spam filters and spam were major problems. In that work, his team began detecting unusual attacks that they later understood were the first APT attacks. The article says this experience launched his cybersecurity career, which has now lasted more than 23 years as the field has grown significantly.

  • Lee’s career path shows that cybersecurity roles can emerge from unexpected backgrounds, including academic research in human viruses and early website-building work.
  • The article highlights that early spam-filtering work exposed Lee’s team to attacks they did not yet understand, which they later recognized as early APT activity.
  • Lee’s current role emphasizes that explaining and externalizing threat research to customers and partners is a major part of cybersecurity work, not just conducting the research itself.
  • The article points to Lee’s use of a sociological lens to examine why some organizations are more resilient than others, showing that security is framed here as partly about organizational behavior.
Key Points
  • 01Martin Lee is the EMEA Lead at Talos, where his work is largely ambassadorial and focused on explaining changes in the threat landscape to customers and partners.
  • 02Lee began his career researching the genetics of human viruses before leaving academia after discovering the internet early on and deciding it would become a major invention in his lifetime.
  • 03After the dot-com crash left him with less work than he wanted, Lee took a job writing spam filters before spam filters and spam itself were major problems.
  • 04While writing spam filters, Lee and his team began detecting unusual attacks that they later understood were among the first APT attacks.
  • 05The article says Lee’s cybersecurity career began largely by accident and has continued for more than 23 years as the field has grown significantly.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a career-profile interview and broad perspective piece, not a report on a specific incident, vulnerability, campaign, or technical finding. It may be interesting for professional development, but it has low operational urgency for security practitioners.

↑ TOP

In this article

Glossary
--helm-command concept
--helm-command is a command-line option that tells a tool which Helm command or executable to use.
.com crash concept
.com crash refers to a failure or disruption involving .com domains or internet naming services.
.NET product
.NET is a Microsoft software framework used to build and run applications, especially on Windows.
also: .NET-based, .NET assembly, recovered .NET assemblies
/accessv2 other
/accessv2 is a web path or endpoint referenced in security reporting.
also: /accessv2 endpoint
0ktapus threat-actor
0ktapus is a threat-actor campaign or group known for large-scale phishing and credential theft targeting identity services.
1.vb tool
1.vb is a Visual Basic script or source file name referenced in security reporting.
146.70.139[.]154 other
146.70.139[.]154 is a defanged IP address indicator used in security reporting to identify network activity without making it clickable.
192.42.116[.]105 other
192.42.116[.]105 is a defanged IP address indicator used in security reporting to identify network activity without making it clickable.
192.42.116[.]58 other
192.42.116[.]58 is a defanged IP address indicator used in security reporting to identify network activity without making it clickable.
2026 Bitdefender Cybersecurity Assessment other
2026 Bitdefender Cybersecurity Assessment is a Bitdefender report or assessment about cybersecurity trends and risks.
2a0a:d683::/32 concept
2a0a:d683::/32 is an IPv6 network range that represents a block of internet addresses.
7Span organization
7Span is a company or organization referenced in security reporting.
also: 7span organization
8Base threat-actor
8Base is a ransomware and data-extortion group known for targeting organizations and leaking stolen data.
AAA virtual server product
An AAA virtual server is a configured service that handles authentication, authorization, and accounting for user access.
aami other
An acronym referenced in security reporting, possibly standing for an organization like the American Association of Medical Instrumentation.
Aanchal Gupta other
Aanchal Gupta is a named person referenced in security or technology reporting.
Abnormal AI organization
Abnormal AI is a company named in the article that provides behavioral AI for security use cases.
Abuse mitigations concept
Abuse mitigations are safeguards that reduce misuse of a service, such as rate limits, blocking, or extra verification.
access token concept
A digital credential that grants temporary access to a protected resource or API on behalf of a user.
also: token
account takeover concept
Account takeover means an attacker gains control of someone else’s account.
also: ATO
Acrobat Reader product
Acrobat Reader is Adobe's software for opening, viewing, and working with PDF documents.
active-directory
Active Directory is Microsoft’s system for managing users, computers, and permissions in many corporate networks.
adminin tool
adminin is a named software package or tool referenced in security reporting.
Administrative dashboard tool
An administrative dashboard is a control panel used by admins to manage a system, users, settings, or data.
Admins concept
Admins are users with elevated permissions to manage systems, accounts, or security settings.
Adobe organization
Adobe is a software company known for products such as Acrobat, Photoshop, Creative Cloud, ColdFusion, and marketing tools.
Adobe Campaign Classic product
Adobe Campaign Classic is Adobe's marketing campaign management platform for creating and managing customer communications.
Adobe ColdFusion product
Adobe ColdFusion is a web application development platform used to build and run dynamic websites and business applications.
Adobe Security Bulletins and Advisories organization
Adobe Security Bulletins and Advisories are official Adobe notices that describe security updates and vulnerabilities in Adobe products.
also: security bulletins
Adobe-hosted instances product
Adobe-hosted instances are versions of Adobe software or services that run on Adobe-managed cloud infrastructure instead of a customer's own servers.
AES protocol
AES is a widely used encryption standard that protects data by turning it into unreadable ciphertext without the right key.
AES-GCM Web Crypto API encryption protocol
AES-GCM Web Crypto API encryption is a browser-supported way to encrypt data using the AES-GCM algorithm.
Aflac Life Insurance Japan organization
Aflac Life Insurance Japan is the Japanese life insurance business associated with Aflac.
agent concept
A small program installed on a device that communicates with a central management system to report health or security data.
Agentic interaction concept
Agentic interaction means an AI system acts more like an autonomous helper that can decide steps and use tools to complete a task.
Agentic Runtime Security concept
A security paradigm that uses autonomous AI agents to detect and respond to threats in real-time during software runtime.
also: Agentic Runtime Security for the Modern Infrastructure, Mitiga’s Agentic Runtime Security
AI concept
AI, or artificial intelligence, is technology that can perform tasks that normally require human reasoning or decision-making.
also: AI tools, AI assistants
AI agents concept
AI agents are software tools that can carry out tasks, sometimes by using other systems or taking actions for a user.
AI chatbots product
AI chatbots are programs that use artificial intelligence to hold conversations with people.
also: LLM chatbot
AI coding agents tool
AI coding agents are software assistants that use artificial intelligence to help write, edit, run, or manage code.
AI coding assistant tool
An AI coding assistant is a tool that helps developers write, explain, edit, or run code using artificial intelligence.
AI crawler tool
An AI crawler is an automated program that visits web pages to collect content for AI systems or AI search products.
also: AI crawlers, AI traffic
AI models concept
AI models are computer systems trained on data so they can recognize patterns, generate content, or make predictions.
also: models
Alexa voice assistant service product
The Alexa voice assistant service is Amazon's voice-controlled system for answering questions, controlling devices, and using online services.
also: Alexa
All Client App types concept
All Client App types is a policy setting that applies rules to every kind of application a user may sign in from.
All Cloud Apps concept
All Cloud Apps is a policy scope that applies a rule to every cloud application covered by the identity system.
All Users concept
All Users is a policy scope meaning the rule applies to every user account in the selected environment.
amazon organization
Amazon is a global technology and e-commerce company.
also: Amazon researchers
AMSI tool
AMSI is a Microsoft Windows security interface that lets antivirus tools inspect scripts and other content before it runs.
Android product
Android is Google's mobile operating system used on many phones, tablets, and other devices.
Andy Jassy other
Andy Jassy is the chief executive officer of Amazon.
also: CEO Andy Jassy
Answer Engine Optimization concept
Answer Engine Optimization is the practice of shaping content so AI answer engines are more likely to find, summarize, or cite it.
also: AEO
anthropic
Anthropic is an AI company known for the Claude family of AI assistants.
Anti-dated commits technique
Anti-dated commits are commits given misleading older dates to make code changes look like they happened earlier than they did.
also: anti-dated January 8 commits
Apache Software Foundation organization
The Apache Software Foundation is a nonprofit organization that supports many widely used open-source software projects.
also: Apache
Apache Tomcat product
Apache Tomcat is an open-source server used to run Java-based web applications.
also: Tomcat
Apache Tomcat 10.1.0-M1 through 10.1.36 product
Apache Tomcat 10.1.0-M1 through 10.1.36 refers to a range of Apache Tomcat server versions affected or discussed in a security context.
Apache Tomcat 11.0.0-M1 through 11.0.4 product
Apache Tomcat 11.0.0-M1 through 11.0.4 refers to a range of Apache Tomcat server versions affected or discussed in a security context.
Apache Tomcat 7.0.0 through 7.0.109 product
Apache Tomcat 7.0.0 through 7.0.109 refers to a range of older Apache Tomcat server versions affected or discussed in a security context.
also: 7.0.x
Apache Tomcat 8.5.0 through 8.5.100 product
Apache Tomcat 8.5.0 through 8.5.100 refers to a range of Apache Tomcat server versions affected or discussed in a security context.
also: 8.5.x
Apache Tomcat 9.0.0.M1 through 9.0.100 product
Apache Tomcat 9.0.0.M1 through 9.0.100 refers to a range of Apache Tomcat server versions affected or discussed in a security context.
Apache Tomcat security team organization
The Apache Tomcat security team is the group responsible for handling security reports and fixes for the Apache Tomcat project.
API protocol
An API is a way for one program to ask another program or service for data or actions.
also: API in Progress LoadMaster, LoadMaster API
API token concept
An API token is a secret value an application uses to prove it is allowed to access another service.
API tokens concept
API tokens are digital keys used to let software access an application or service.
API-driven servers concept
API-driven servers are systems mainly controlled through software interfaces rather than manual clicks or direct user interaction.
also: backend servers, payload servers
Application and business transaction records concept
Application and business transaction records are logs or stored details about what software and business systems have done.
AppOmni organization
AppOmni is a cybersecurity company focused on securing software-as-a-service applications.
AppOmni 2025 organization
AppOmni 2025 appears to refer to a 2025 report, product release, or security research item from AppOmni, a SaaS security company.
also: AppOmni's 2025 research
APT attacks technique
APT attacks are long-running, targeted cyber intrusions usually carried out by skilled and well-resourced groups.
apt28 threat-actor
APT28 is a Russian state-linked hacking group also known as Fancy Bear and associated with military intelligence operations.
Aptos protocol
Aptos is a blockchain network used for cryptocurrency transactions and decentralized applications.
Aqua Security organization
Aqua Security is a company that makes tools for protecting cloud, container, and Kubernetes environments.
Arbitrary code execution vulnerability
Arbitrary code execution means an attacker can make a system run commands or programs of the attacker's choosing.
arbitrary file read vulnerability
Arbitrary file read is a vulnerability that lets an attacker read files they should not be allowed to access.
also: unauthenticated, arbitrary file read
Argo CD tool
Argo CD is a Kubernetes deployment tool that keeps applications in sync with configuration stored in Git.
also: ArgoCD
Argo Project organization
Argo Project is the open-source organization and project family behind Kubernetes tools such as Argo CD.
argo-cd-repo-server tool
argo-cd-repo-server is an Argo CD component that works with Git repositories and generates application manifests for deployment.
argo-cdown tool
argo-cdown is a named software package or tool referenced in security reporting.
Argos organization
Argos is a UK retailer that sells general merchandise and consumer products online and in stores.
ARTBrowser tool
ARTBrowser is a named browser-like tool or malware component referenced in security reporting.
ARToken tool
ARToken is a named token, tool, or component referenced in security reporting.
also: ARToken Panel
ARTSender tool
ARTSender is a named tool or malware component referenced in security reporting.
AS32167 concept
AS32167 is an Autonomous System Number that identifies a particular internet network for routing purposes.
ASNs concept
ASNs are unique numbers that identify networks on the internet so traffic can be routed between them.
aspnet_compiler tool
aspnet_compiler is a Microsoft ASP.NET tool used to precompile web applications and sometimes abused by attackers as a trusted executable.
asyncrat
AsyncRAT is a remote access trojan that lets attackers control infected Windows computers and steal information.
also: AsyncRAT module
Attacker-supplied workload technique
An attacker-supplied workload is code, a container, or a job provided by an attacker for a system to run.
Attribution Business Insights product
Attribution Business Insights is a named dashboard or analytics feature referenced in reporting about tracking traffic or attribution.
Attribution Business Insights dashboard product
Attribution Business Insights dashboard is a named analytics dashboard for viewing traffic or attribution information.
also: dashboard
authentication concept
Authentication is the process of proving that a person or system is allowed to log in.
Authentication Broker tool
An authentication broker is a service that helps applications handle user sign-ins through an identity provider.
also: WAM
authentication bypass vulnerability
Authentication bypass is a flaw or technique that lets someone access a system without properly logging in.
also: bypass
authentication credentials concept
Authentication credentials are information such as passwords, tokens, or keys used to prove someone is allowed to access a system.
authentication data concept
Authentication data is information used to verify identity, such as passwords, tokens, cookies, or keys.
Authorization concept
Authorization is the process of deciding what a logged-in user or system is allowed to access or do.
authorization endpoint protocol
An authorization endpoint is the web address where an app sends a user to sign in and approve access.
authorization server concept
An authorization server is the system that checks a user's identity and issues access tokens for apps or services.
automatic sync concept
Automatic sync is when software updates or copies data between systems without a person manually starting it each time.
autonomous agents concept
Autonomous agents are software or AI systems that can take actions toward a goal with limited human guidance.
axios tool
Axios is a JavaScript library developers use to make web requests from applications.
azur-email-com other
azur-email-com is a domain-like indicator referenced in security reporting.
Azure command-line interface tool
The Azure command-line interface is Microsoft's command-line tool for managing Azure cloud resources.
also: Microsoft's Azure command-line interface, Azure CLI application
azure-cli tool
azure-cli is Microsoft's command-line tool for managing Azure cloud resources from a terminal.
azure-functions product
azure-functions is a software package or project related to Microsoft Azure Functions, a service for running code without managing servers.
Banco Santander organization
Banco Santander is a large international banking and financial services organization based in Spain.
Bandicam product
Bandicam is a screen recording software product for Windows.
Bandwidth concept
Bandwidth is the amount of data a network connection can carry in a given time.
bank accounts concept
Bank accounts are financial accounts at a bank used to store, receive, and send money.
Base64 concept
A method to convert binary data into text using 64 characters, often used to obfuscate malware scripts or passwords.
Benefits enrollment and limited claims data concept
Benefits enrollment and limited claims data are records about employee benefit sign-ups and some related insurance or healthcare claims.
Bert-Jan Pals other
Bert-Jan Pals is a named person referenced in security reporting.
also: Pals
Binds technique
Binds are operations that connect or authenticate to a directory or network service.
also: LDAP binds, GSSAPI-authenticated binds
Bing product
Bing is Microsoft’s search engine.
BioShocking AI tool
BioShocking AI is a named tool or service referenced in security reporting, likely connected to artificial intelligence activity.
bitcoin
Bitcoin is a decentralized digital currency often used for payments, investment, and sometimes ransomware demands.
bitdefender organization
Bitdefender is a cybersecurity company known for antivirus and other security products.
Bitdefender Labs organization
Bitdefender Labs is the research team of cybersecurity company Bitdefender.
bitlocker
BitLocker is Microsoft’s disk encryption feature that protects data on Windows devices if they are lost or stolen.
Blackfield ransomware malware
Blackfield ransomware is a ransomware family referenced in security reporting.
BleepingComputer organization
BleepingComputer is a publication that covers technology and computer security topics.
Blockchain and public RPC infrastructure protocol
Blockchain and public RPC infrastructure are public services that let apps and users read from or send transactions to blockchain networks.
also: public RPC infrastructure
Blocklists tool
Blocklists are lists of known bad domains, IP addresses, files, or senders that security tools block.
Blogger product
Blogger is Google's blogging platform used to create and publish websites.
also: Blogger pages
Blogspot product
Blogspot is Google's older blogging domain often used to host simple websites or blogs.
also: blogspot.com
BNB Smart Chain protocol
BNB Smart Chain is a blockchain network used for cryptocurrency transactions and decentralized applications.
Bot operator threat-actor
A bot operator is a person or group that controls automated accounts or programs online.
also: bot operators
Bot traffic concept
Bot traffic is website or network activity generated by automated programs rather than people.
also: automated bots, bots
Brand Impersonation technique
Brand impersonation is when attackers pretend to be a trusted company or service to trick people.
brazil other
Brazil is a country in South America.
breach and attack simulation concept
An automated security testing platform that safely simulates real-world attacks to see how well an organization's defenses hold up.
Browser exploitation technique
Browser exploitation is attacking weaknesses in a web browser to run code, steal data, or escape protections.
Browser sandboxing technique
Browser sandboxing is a safety design that limits what web pages can do to the rest of your device.
also: browser sandboxing limits
Browser-only ransomware malware
Browser-only ransomware is ransomware-like malicious activity that runs in a web browser rather than installing a traditional program on the device.
business-email-compromise technique
Business email compromise is a scam where attackers use or impersonate business email accounts to trick people into sending money or data.
also: BEC
Buy Now Pay Later concept
Buy Now Pay Later is a payment option that lets shoppers receive goods immediately and pay for them later, often in installments.
also: BNPL, Buy Now Pay Later (BNPL)
Buy Now Pay Later fraud concept
Buy Now Pay Later fraud is abuse of installment-payment services using stolen identities, fake accounts, or unauthorized purchases.
C2 communication concept
C2 communication is the link malware uses to receive instructions from an attacker and send information back.
C:\Users\Public directory concept
The C:\Users\Public directory is a shared Windows folder that all local users can usually access.
Caesars Entertainment organization
Caesars Entertainment is a major casino and hospitality company.
Caltech organization
Caltech is a well-known U.S. research university formally called the California Institute of Technology.
Campaign Classic product
Campaign Classic is an Adobe marketing campaign management product used to manage customer communications.
also: Adobe Campaign, Campaign Classic marketing automation platform
canada
Canada is a North American country often referenced in cybersecurity reporting as a target, regulator, or law-enforcement partner.
cap.ps1 tool
cap.ps1 is a PowerShell script file name referenced in security reporting.
CAPTCHA concept
A test that tells humans and computers apart, often by asking you to click on pictures or type distorted letters.
Casbaneiro malware
Casbaneiro is a Latin American banking-trojan malware family used to steal financial and online banking information.
Cato AI Labs organization
Cato AI Labs is a research group associated with Cato Networks that focuses on AI and cybersecurity analysis.
Center for AI Standards and Innovation organization
Center for AI Standards and Innovation is a U.S. government center focused on AI standards and related technology policy.
CEO Coaching International organization
CEO Coaching International is a business coaching organization referenced in security reporting.
ceo-coachinginc[.]com other
ceo-coachinginc[.]com is a defanged domain indicator referenced in security reporting.
chat tools product
Chat tools are apps or services used for real-time messaging and collaboration.
chatbots concept
A computer program that simulates human conversation to answer questions or perform tasks.
Check Point Research organization
Check Point Research is the threat intelligence and research team of cybersecurity company Check Point.
also: Check Point, Israeli cybersecurity company
Check Point VPN product
Check Point VPN is a virtual private network product or feature from Check Point used for secure remote access.
Checkmarx organization
Checkmarx is a cybersecurity company known for tools that scan software code for security vulnerabilities.
chief security officer concept
A chief security officer is a senior leader responsible for an organization’s security.
also: CSO
Children's privacy laws concept
Children's privacy laws are rules meant to protect children's personal information and online privacy.
china
China is a country often referenced in cybersecurity reporting involving technology, espionage, regulation, and threat activity.
ChocoPoC tool
ChocoPoC is a named proof-of-concept or security tool referenced in security reporting.
also: ChocoRAT
Chrome product
A popular web browser developed by Google, frequently targeted by exploit kits and phishing campaigns.
chrome-extensions
Chrome extensions are add-ons that change or enhance the Google Chrome browser, but malicious ones can steal data or spy on users.
also: Chrome extension
Chromium product
Chromium is the open-source browser project that underlies Google Chrome and several other browsers.
also: Chromium-based browsers
cisa organization
CISA is the U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, which helps protect critical infrastructure and shares security guidance.
cisco-talos
Cisco Talos is Cisco’s threat intelligence and security research group.
Citrix organization
Citrix is a company known for remote access, virtualization, and application delivery products.
City of London Police organization
The City of London Police is a UK police force with a major role in fraud and cybercrime reporting and investigation.
Civil penalty concept
A civil penalty is a fine or punishment imposed for breaking a law or regulation without treating it as a criminal case.
Classifier tool
A classifier is a system that sorts data into categories, such as labeling messages as spam or not spam.
also: safety filter
claude
Claude is Anthropic’s family of AI assistants and language models.
Claude Cowork product
Claude Cowork is a named AI-related product or feature referenced in reporting.
Claude Fable 5 product
Claude Fable 5 is a named AI-related product or model referenced in reporting.
also: Fable
Claude Mythos product
Claude Mythos is a named AI-related product or model referenced in reporting.
Claude Mythos 5 product
Claude Mythos 5 is a named AI model or model variant referenced in reporting about Claude-style language models.
Claude Platform product
The Claude Platform is Anthropic's set of AI services and tools for using Claude models in apps and workflows.
claude-code
Claude Code is Anthropic’s AI coding assistant for helping developers write and manage software projects.
claude-opus-4-8 product
claude-opus-4-8 is a model identifier that appears to refer to an Opus 4.8 Claude-style AI model.
Claude.ai product
Claude.ai is Anthropic's website for using the Claude AI assistant.
clawdbot tool
A software bot referenced in security reporting, likely used for automation or malicious purposes.
ClawHub tool
ClawHub is a named tool, service, or project referenced in security reporting.
also: skill hub, skill repository
ClearFake malware
ClearFake is a malware delivery campaign that uses compromised websites and fake browser-update messages to trick users into running malicious code.
Click & Collect concept
Click & Collect is a shopping option where a customer buys online and picks up the order in a store or collection point.
also: click-and-click
Click & Collect concept
Click & Collect is a shopping option where a customer buys online and picks up the order in a store or collection point.
clickfake-interview technique
ClickFake Interview is a named campaign or lure referenced in security reporting that tricks people with fake interview-related content.
clickfix
ClickFix is a social-engineering tactic that tricks users into copying and running malicious commands to “fix” a fake problem.
client application concept
A client application is software that requests access to a service on behalf of a user or system.
also: application
clipboard concept
The clipboard is the temporary place where copied text, images, or files are stored before pasting.
Clop threat-actor
Clop is a ransomware group and malware family known for stealing data and extorting victims.
cloud concept
The cloud is internet-based computing where data and applications run on remote servers instead of only on your own device.
also: cloud systems
Cloud Security Data Lake product
A Cloud Security Data Lake is a centralized cloud repository that stores large amounts of security data for investigation, analytics, and detection.
cloudflare
Cloudflare is a company that provides content delivery, DDoS protection, DNS, and web security services.
Cloudflare Bot Management product
Cloudflare Bot Management is a Cloudflare product that detects and controls automated bot traffic to websites.
also: Bot Management
Cloudflare Workers product
Cloudflare Workers is a Cloudflare product that lets developers run small pieces of code on Cloudflare’s global network.
also: Workers
CNAPP product
CNAPP, or Cloud-Native Application Protection Platform, is a type of security product that helps protect cloud applications from development through runtime.
co-op organization
Co-op is a British consumer cooperative and retail organization.
Code concept
Code is the written instructions that tell software or computers what to do.
Code-generating models concept
Code-generating models are AI systems that can write or suggest computer code from prompts.
also: AI coding tools
CodeQL tool
CodeQL is GitHub's code analysis tool that searches source code for bugs and security vulnerabilities.
codfish-semantic-release-action tool
codfish-semantic-release-action is a GitHub Action related to automating software releases with semantic-release.
ColdFusion product
ColdFusion is Adobe's platform for building and running server-side web applications.
also: ColdFusion web app development platform
command injection technique
Command injection is an attack where someone tricks an application into running system commands chosen by the attacker.
command-and-control concept
Command-and-control is the way attackers remotely send instructions to malware and receive data back from infected machines.
also: C2
Company F organization
Company F is an anonymized organization name used in reporting or legal documents.
also: luxury-jewelry retailer
Company H organization
Company H is an anonymized organization name used in reporting or legal documents.
also: online-communication platform
compiled native Python extension concept
A compiled native Python extension is a Python add-on built from lower-level code, such as C or C++, that runs as machine code.
also: extension
compromised credentials concept
Compromised credentials are login details that have been stolen, exposed, or otherwise made unsafe.
also: valid credentials
compromised password combo lists concept
Compromised password combo lists are stolen username-and-password collections that attackers use to try logging in to other sites.
computer intrusion concept
Computer intrusion is unauthorized access to a computer, account, or network.
also: intrusions
Conditional Access Policies concept
Conditional Access Policies are rules that decide when someone is allowed to sign in, such as requiring MFA or a managed device.
also: Conditional Access Policy, CAP, CAPs
ConnectWise organization
ConnectWise is a software vendor known for remote management and IT service provider tools.
conspiracy concept
Conspiracy is an agreement between people to commit an unlawful act.
Contagious Interview technique
Contagious Interview is a North Korea-linked campaign that tricks job seekers or developers into running malicious software during fake interviews.
also: Contagious Interview / Famous Chollima
Content Independence Day concept
Content Independence Day is a named campaign or event referenced in reporting about control over web content and AI or search traffic.
also: Content Independence Day in 2025
Content Independence Day 2025 concept
Content Independence Day 2025 is a named campaign or event referenced in reporting about web content and traffic control.
Content pages concept
Content pages are web pages that publish information such as articles, product descriptions, or documentation.
Control plane concept
A control plane is the part of a system that manages configuration, decisions, and coordination rather than carrying user data.
copilots product
Copilots are AI assistants built into software to help users write, search, summarize, or complete tasks.
Corporate payment card information concept
Corporate payment card information is business credit or debit card data that can be misused for unauthorized purchases.
Crawl-to-referral ratio concept
Crawl-to-referral ratio compares how often automated crawlers visit a site with how much visitor traffic they send back.
also: crawl-to-referral ratios
Crawler tool
A crawler is an automated program that visits web pages to collect or index information.
also: crawlers
credential and token spray attacks technique
Credential and token spray attacks try the same passwords or access tokens across many accounts to avoid obvious repeated failures on one account.
credential stuffing attacks technique
Credential stuffing attacks try stolen username-and-password pairs on many sites to break into accounts where people reused passwords.
also: Credential Stuff Attacks
credential theft technique
Credential theft is the stealing of usernames, passwords, tokens, or other login information.
credential-stuffing technique
Credential stuffing is when attackers try stolen usernames and passwords on many websites to see where they still work.
credentials concept
Credentials are login details or access tokens that prove a user or system is allowed to access something.
also: stolen Fortinet credentials, exposed credentials, harvested credentials
Credit card numbers concept
Credit card numbers are payment card identifiers that criminals try to steal for fraud.
Credit monitoring service product
A credit monitoring service watches a person's credit reports for changes that may signal identity theft or fraud.
also: Credit Monitoring
criminal complaint concept
A criminal complaint is a formal legal document accusing someone of a crime.
also: complaint
Criminal IP product
Criminal IP is a cybersecurity search and threat-intelligence service used to look up information about internet-connected assets and risks.
Criminal IP Connector tool
Criminal IP Connector is a tool or integration associated with the Criminal IP threat intelligence service.
critical infrastructure concept
Critical infrastructure refers to essential systems and services that societies and organizations depend on.
crowdstrike organization
CrowdStrike is a cybersecurity company known for endpoint protection, threat intelligence, and incident response.
CrowdStrike 2026 organization
CrowdStrike 2026 appears to refer to a 2026 report, product release, or security research item from CrowdStrike.
cryptocurrency concept
Cryptocurrency is digital money, such as Bitcoin, that uses cryptography and usually runs on a blockchain.
Cryptocurrency seed phrases concept
Cryptocurrency seed phrases are secret recovery words that can give full access to a crypto wallet.
Current user's context concept
Current user's context means the permissions, identity, and environment of the user account that is currently running something.
Currys organization
Currys is a UK and Ireland electronics retailer.
cursor
Cursor is an AI-assisted code editor built for software developers.
cursorsandbox tool
cursorsandbox is a Cursor application helper component referenced in security reporting.
CurXecute vulnerability
CurXecute is a named vulnerability, exploit, or attack technique referenced in security reporting involving Cursor-related execution behavior.
custom packet-sniffing tool tool
A custom packet-sniffing tool is specially made software used to capture and inspect network data packets.
also: packet-sniffing tool
CVE vulnerability
A CVE is a public identifier used to track a specific known software vulnerability.
CVE database tool
The CVE database is a public catalog of known cybersecurity vulnerabilities with standard tracking IDs.
CVSS score concept
A CVSS score is a standard number used to show how severe a software vulnerability is.
also: CVSS
CVSS v3.1 concept
CVSS v3.1 is a standard scoring system used to describe how serious a software vulnerability is.
cyber intrusion concept
A cyber intrusion is an unauthorized break-in or access attempt against computers, accounts, or networks.
Cybersecurity concept
Cybersecurity is the practice of protecting computers, networks, software, and data from digital attacks or misuse.
cybersecurity-and-infrastructure-security-agency organization
The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency is the U.S. government agency that helps defend critical infrastructure and improve cybersecurity.
Cycode organization
Cycode is a software supply-chain and application security company.
CyCognito organization
CyCognito is a cybersecurity company known for external attack surface management technology.
CyCognito Platform product
The CyCognito Platform is an external attack surface management product used to find and assess internet-exposed assets.
Dario Amodei other
Dario Amodei is the co-founder and chief executive officer of Anthropic, an AI company.
also: CEO Dario Amodei
Dark patterns concept
Dark patterns are website or app designs that manipulate people into doing things they might not otherwise choose, such as subscribing or sharing data.
data breach concept
A data breach is an incident where sensitive, private, or protected information is accessed, exposed, or stolen without permission.
also: breach
data center concept
A data center is a facility that houses servers and networking equipment for running digital services.
data exfiltration technique
Data exfiltration is the unauthorized removal or copying of data from a system.
also: exfiltrates
data extortion technique
A crime where attackers steal sensitive files and threaten to publish or sell them unless a ransom is paid.
data extortion groups threat-actor
Data extortion groups are criminal groups that steal information and threaten to leak it unless they are paid.
Dates of birth concept
Dates of birth are personal information that can help identify someone and may be sensitive if exposed.
David Sacks other
David Sacks is a technology entrepreneur and investor who is often referenced in business and technology reporting.
also: Former AI czar David Sacks
dayjs product
dayjs is a legitimate and widely used JavaScript library.
DCloud Uni-App tool
DCloud Uni-App is a development framework for building apps that can run across multiple platforms.
DeepSeek organization
DeepSeek is an AI service provider referenced in the article.
also: DeepSeek models
deepseek_python_20260125_da0631.py tool
deepseek_python_20260125_da0631.py is a Python script file name referenced in security reporting.
Defender Experts product
Defender Experts is a Microsoft security service where specialists help monitor and respond to threats.
Deflate protocol
Deflate is a common data-compression method used to make files or network data smaller.
Defused organization
Defused is the threat intelligence company named in the article.
denial-of-service
Denial-of-service is an attack that overwhelms a system so legitimate users cannot access it.
also: DoS
Department of Commerce organization
The Department of Commerce is a U.S. government department that handles trade, industry, and related policy.
Department of Homeland Security organization
The Department of Homeland Security is a U.S. government department responsible for domestic security, including cyber defense.
Department of Justice organization
The main U.S. federal law enforcement agency responsible for prosecuting federal crimes and upholding justice.
Dependency Review tool
Dependency Review is a GitHub feature that checks software dependencies for risky or vulnerable packages in pull requests.
dependents concept
Dependents are people, such as family members, who are covered under another person's benefits or records.
Deployment cache concept
A deployment cache is stored deployment data used to speed up or reuse application releases.
also: stored deployment data, cached deployment data
DEV#POPPER threat-actor
DEV#POPPER is a named threat activity cluster referenced in security reporting.
device-code-phishing technique
Device-code phishing tricks users into entering a legitimate login code that gives attackers access to their account.
also: device code attacks
Direct deposit bank account information concept
Direct deposit bank account information is the banking data used to send paychecks or payments directly into an account.
Directory server tool
A directory server is a system that stores and provides information about users, devices, and permissions for a network.
also: external directory server
Directory-backed identity concept
Directory-backed identity means user accounts and permissions are managed through a central directory service such as LDAP or Active Directory.
discord product
Discord is a communication platform for text, voice, and video chats, popular with gaming and online communities.
Discord tokens concept
Discord tokens are secret account or bot credentials that can let someone access Discord services as that user or bot.
DKIM protocol
DKIM is an email signing method that helps verify that a message was authorized by a domain and was not changed after signing.
DLL sideloading technique
DLL sideloading is an attack where malware places a fake DLL where a trusted Windows program will load it.
dll-side-loading
DLL side-loading is when attackers trick a legitimate program into loading a malicious DLL file.
DMARC protocol
DMARC is an email policy check that uses SPF or DKIM alignment to help determine whether a message claiming to come from a domain should be trusted.
DNS Jumper tool
DNS Jumper is a Windows utility for changing DNS server settings.
DNS Proxy tool
A DNS proxy is a server or feature that forwards domain-name lookup requests between clients and DNS resolvers.
DNS recursive resolver deployment technique
DNS recursive resolver deployment means setting up servers that look up domain names for users by querying other DNS servers as needed.
DOMPurify tool
DOMPurify is a JavaScript library that cleans unsafe HTML to help prevent cross-site scripting attacks.
downloader malware
A downloader is malware or a small program that retrieves and installs additional malicious code from the internet.
downloadfix technique
downloadfix is a named lure, tool, or campaign label referenced in security reporting that suggests a fake fix involving downloads.
Downloads-folder method technique
The Downloads-folder method is a social-engineering technique that tells victims to run something from their Downloads folder.
Drive-by compromise technique
A drive-by compromise is an attack where a device can be infected just by visiting a malicious or compromised website.
Driver's license or other government ID numbers concept
Driver's license or other government ID numbers are official identifiers that can be sensitive if exposed or stolen.
DS4Windows tool
DS4Windows is a tool that lets Windows computers use PlayStation controllers like standard game controllers.
DuneSlide malware
DuneSlide is a named malware family or threat tool referenced in security reporting.
durabletask tool
durabletask is a software framework for building reliable long-running workflows that can pause, resume, and coordinate tasks.
Dynamic stage generation technique
Dynamic stage generation is a technique where malware creates the next part of its attack code during execution instead of storing it plainly beforehand.
East Midlands Cyber Resilience Center organization
The East Midlands Cyber Resilience Center is a regional cyber resilience organization referenced in security reporting.
East Midlands Cyber Resilience Centre organization
The East Midlands Cyber Resilience Centre is a UK regional organization that helps businesses improve cyber resilience.
easy-day-js malware
easy-day-js is the malicious dependency named in the article.
ECDLP-256 concept
ECDLP-256 refers to a 256-bit elliptic-curve discrete logarithm problem, a hard math problem used as the basis for some cryptography.
EDR product
EDR is security software that monitors computers and servers for suspicious activity and helps respond to attacks.
